EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north into Deep East Texas and the southern sections of North Central Louisiana by afternoon on April 19th, ahead of a vigorous upper level trough that ejected east from New Mexico across Texas. Large scale forcing increased by afternoon ahead of the trough as it approached the expanding warm sector, characteristic of moderate instability and strong wind shear. Areas of showers and thunderstorms developed and became widespread near and north of the warm front, and limited the northward progress of the front, but severe thunderstorms still developed within the warm sector across Deep East Texas and Northcentral Louisiana, resulting in areas of damaging winds across these areas. An isolated elevated severe thunderstorm also developed over portions of Lamar County Texas, which moved into Red River County and produced quarter size hail near Clarksville. These storms eventually weakened during the mid evening hours with the passage of the trough through the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down across Highway 184 near Hemphill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak shortwave trough shifted east across East Texas and North Louisiana during the morning hours of April 28th, where large scale forcing contributed to the development of showers and thunderstorms over East Texas and North Louisiana. Cold air aloft associated with this trough resulted in steep lapse rates such that isolated severe thunderstorms developed over portions of East Texas throughout the morning and afternoon, where instances of large hail were reported. These storms diminished during the late afternoon, but a deepening upper level trough sweeping southeast across Oklahoma and Arkansas reinforced a cold front southeast across the Southern Plains and Ozarks region, where a complex of showers and thunderstorms developed by mid and late afternoon over these areas and quickly progressed southeast into the Four State Region. Despite the showers and thunderstorms that affected the area earlier in the day, instability remained adequate especially over East Texas, Southeast Oklahoma, and adjacent sections of Southwest Arkansas. As large scale forcing increased ahead of the trough, thus tapping into this unstable air, a squall line progressed southeast through all of the region during the late evening through the early morning hours on April 29th, producing widespread reports of damaging winds and large hail. A couple of isolated tornadoes also touched down across McCurtain County Oklahoma, one associated with the supercell that developed ahead of the line, and another associated within the squall line itself. These storms exited Deep East Texas and the southern sections of Northcentral Louisiana into Southeast Texas and South Louisiana by 4 AM.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed along Appleby Sand Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early spring cold air mass settled into the region on Easter Sunday and lingered around the region through the 13th and 14th. A passing mid level storm system provided enough atmospheric lift to generate widespread snowfall across the extreme southern Texas Panhandle into the northern South Plains. Friona (Parmer County) saw heavy snow for a period of time on the evening of the 13th reporting five inches of snow. Elsewhere, generally one to three inches of snow fell.||Snowfall reports below are from NWS cooperative weather observers or are public reports.||5.0 inches at Friona (Parmer County), 3.5 inches at Silverton (Briscoe County), 2.0 inches at Memphis (Hall County), 2.0 inches at Turkey (Hall County), 2.0 inches at Dimmitt (Castro County), 1.5 inches at Tulia (Swisher County), 1.5 inches at Estelline 3S (Hall County), 1.5 inches at Childress 7NW (Childress County), 1.3 inches at Childress (Childress County), 1.5 inches at Olton (Lamb County), 1.5 inches at Plainview (Hale County), 1.5 inches at Roaring Springs (Motley County), 1.0 inch at Muleshoe (Bailey County), and 1.0 inch at Floydada 9SE (Floyd County).

A southerly return flow commenced throughout the day on April 24th, in wake of a cold frontal passage that had occurred a couple of days prior on the 22nd. However, low level moisture return was meager, but a sharply amplifying upper level trough over the Central Plains and Midwest region dug south into the Red River Valley of Southern Oklahoma and North Texas during the afternoon. Strong large scale forcing occurred ahead of this trough over Eastern and Southern Oklahoma into Northeast Texas, along and ahead of a reinforcing cold front that shifted southeast over these areas. Strong wind shear also developed ahead of the trough during the afternoon and evening, with very steep lapse rates noted beneath the trough over the Red River Valley into Northeast Texas. This contributed to the development of scattered severe thunderstorms during the afternoon over portions of Southern and Southeast Oklahoma and Northeast Texas, with several supercell thunderstorms developing and tracking southeast over these areas through the evening, and eventually into Northern Louisiana during the mid and late evening hours. Numerous reports of large hail were reported from these storms, with some isolated instances of damaging winds and funnel clouds across portions of Northeast Texas. Multiple cell mergers contributed to an extremely severe supercell with a large swath of destructive straight line winds in excess of 80-90 mph developing over Southern Morris and much of Cass Counties, which accelerated southeast into portions of Northwest Louisiana by mid-evening, thus ending the severe threat for Northeast Texas with the cold frontal passage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell just southwest of Nesbitt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds aloft mixed to the surface following the passage of a cold front. From late morning until early evening, numerous reports of sustained winds above 40 MPH and gusts as high as 62 MPH or greater were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The JA Ranch West Texas Mesonet station 4 miles east of Palo Duro measured sustained winds in the 40 to 42 MPH range during this time period. The highest sustained wind of 42 MPH occurred at 16:54.

A southerly return flow commenced throughout the day on April 24th, in wake of a cold frontal passage that had occurred a couple of days prior on the 22nd. However, low level moisture return was meager, but a sharply amplifying upper level trough over the Central Plains and Midwest region dug south into the Red River Valley of Southern Oklahoma and North Texas during the afternoon. Strong large scale forcing occurred ahead of this trough over Eastern and Southern Oklahoma into Northeast Texas, along and ahead of a reinforcing cold front that shifted southeast over these areas. Strong wind shear also developed ahead of the trough during the afternoon and evening, with very steep lapse rates noted beneath the trough over the Red River Valley into Northeast Texas. This contributed to the development of scattered severe thunderstorms during the afternoon over portions of Southern and Southeast Oklahoma and Northeast Texas, with several supercell thunderstorms developing and tracking southeast over these areas through the evening, and eventually into Northern Louisiana during the mid and late evening hours. Numerous reports of large hail were reported from these storms, with some isolated instances of damaging winds and funnel clouds across portions of Northeast Texas. Multiple cell mergers contributed to an extremely severe supercell with a large swath of destructive straight line winds in excess of 80-90 mph developing over Southern Morris and much of Cass Counties, which accelerated southeast into portions of Northwest Louisiana by mid-evening, thus ending the severe threat for Northeast Texas with the cold frontal passage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rapidly rotating funnel cloud was observed near Talco.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds aloft mixed to the surface following the passage of a cold front. From late morning until early evening, numerous reports of sustained winds above 40 MPH and gusts as high as 62 MPH or greater were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Texas Mesonet station 1 mile south-southwest of Panhandle measured sustained winds in the 40 to 41 MPH range during this time period. The highest sustained wind of 41 MPH occurred at 14:59.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon along a surface low pressure trough. One thunderstorm over the southeast Texas Panhandle produced severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to quarter size hail fell east of Wellington on U.S. Highway 203.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense surface low pressure center north of the Texas and Oklahoma Panhandles moved from southeast Colorado into southwest Kansas during the late morning and early afternoon. The associated strong pressure gradient across the Panhandles resulted in numerous reports of high winds across the western two-thirds of the Texas Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This gust was measured at the West Texas Mesonet station 4 miles west-northwest of Stratford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense surface low pressure center north of the Texas and Oklahoma Panhandles moved from southeast Colorado into southwest Kansas during the late morning and early afternoon. The associated strong pressure gradient across the Panhandles resulted in numerous reports of high winds across the western two-thirds of the Texas Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This gust was measured at Dalhart Municipal Airport, located 3 miles northwest of Dalhart.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak shortwave trough shifted east across East Texas and North Louisiana during the morning hours of April 28th, where large scale forcing contributed to the development of showers and thunderstorms over East Texas and North Louisiana. Cold air aloft associated with this trough resulted in steep lapse rates such that isolated severe thunderstorms developed over portions of East Texas throughout the morning and afternoon, where instances of large hail were reported. These storms diminished during the late afternoon, but a deepening upper level trough sweeping southeast across Oklahoma and Arkansas reinforced a cold front southeast across the Southern Plains and Ozarks region, where a complex of showers and thunderstorms developed by mid and late afternoon over these areas and quickly progressed southeast into the Four State Region. Despite the showers and thunderstorms that affected the area earlier in the day, instability remained adequate especially over East Texas, Southeast Oklahoma, and adjacent sections of Southwest Arkansas. As large scale forcing increased ahead of the trough, thus tapping into this unstable air, a squall line progressed southeast through all of the region during the late evening through the early morning hours on April 29th, producing widespread reports of damaging winds and large hail. A couple of isolated tornadoes also touched down across McCurtain County Oklahoma, one associated with the supercell that developed ahead of the line, and another associated within the squall line itself. These storms exited Deep East Texas and the southern sections of Northcentral Louisiana into Southeast Texas and South Louisiana by 4 AM.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down on Highway 103.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much needed rainfall fell across areas where exceptional drought had persisted (mostly in southern Zapata County) during mid April. Unfortunately, most of the remainder of Deep South Texas has received less than one quarter of an inch through the same time period, especially across the Rio Grande Valley, where April temperatures remained well above normal. Overall average temperatures through April ranked between the third to fifth warmest in recorded history between Brownsville, Harlingen, and McAllen, after temperatures in March notched the record warmest at all three locations. For all of April up until this point...the majority of rainfall has fallen across the northern ranchlands of Deep South Texas and the northern edge of the Rio Grande Valley. These areas received anywhere between generally 50 to 110 percent of their normal rainfall. The rest of Deep South Texas, including most of the Rio Grande Valley, have received between 5 to 50 percent of their normal rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall during mid April was sufficient to improve drought conditions in Starr County, dropping the area of Exceptional (D4) Drought to Extreme (D3) Drought starting April 21st and continuing through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: April 2020 picked up where March left off - much above average temperatures and dry conditions - which allowed  drought conditions ranging from severe (mainly east of Interstate 69C) to extreme (mainly west of Interstate 69C) to continue to begin the month. On the 3rd and 4th, however, thunderstorms tracked from the ranchlands of northern Zapata into southern Jim Hogg/southern Brooks, then through northern Hidalgo and Willacy County, and improvements would begin to show up by April 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) Drought persisted in northern Cameron County, with a slice of Extreme (D3) Drought from Brownsville to Los Fresnos in southern Cameron County, through the month of April 2020, as welcome rainfall for other parts of the Rio Grande Valley and Deep S. Texas missed most of the county - though just enough fell to preclude any worsening until May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak shortwave trough shifted east across East Texas and North Louisiana during the morning hours of April 28th, where large scale forcing contributed to the development of showers and thunderstorms over East Texas and North Louisiana. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early spring cold air mass settled into the region on Easter Sunday and lingered around the region through the 13th and 14th. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions developed throughout much of the month of January across Rusk and Gregg Counties in East Texas, in wake of a very dry November-December 2019 period that was observed across these areas. In fact, the prolonged dryness resulted in the Sabine River basin (including portions of these two counties) experiencing below normal streamflows through much of the month. However, the frequency of rainfall events increased across East Texas during the middle and latter half of January, with monthly rainfall amounts ranging between four and five inches, which was one to two inches above normal. This rain, the resultant runoff it produced, and the improved streamflows across the Sabine River Basin, resulted in a one category drought improvement to Moderate Drought (D1) across much of Rusk and Gregg Counties to end the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front moved south through the Ark-La-Tex to the Texas and Louisiana coasts on the morning of April 18th, although this front was shallow with its remnant elevated front stalling along the I-20 corridor of East Texas and North Louisiana. Elevated moisture return commenced during the afternoon hours atop this shallow post-frontal air mass, as weak shortwave energy ejected east northeast over the region ahead of an upper trough over the Four Corners Region. Ample instability aloft remained ahead of this approaching disturbance, with isolated to widely scattered showers and thunderstorms developing during the late afternoon and early evening hours. One of these storms briefly became severe, producing quarter size hail north of Cushing in Southern Rusk County. Additional scattered showers and thunderstorms developed overnight ahead of a northward moving warm front, but these remained sub-severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported northeast of Winfield. Report from mPING.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north into Deep East Texas and the southern sections of North Central Louisiana by afternoon on April 19th, ahead of a vigorous upper level trough that ejected east from New Mexico across Texas. Large scale forcing increased by afternoon ahead of the trough as it approached the expanding warm sector, characteristic of moderate instability and strong wind shear. Areas of showers and thunderstorms developed and became widespread near and north of the warm front, and limited the northward progress of the front, but severe thunderstorms still developed within the warm sector across Deep East Texas and Northcentral Louisiana, resulting in areas of damaging winds across these areas. An isolated elevated severe thunderstorm also developed over portions of Lamar County Texas, which moved into Red River County and produced quarter size hail near Clarksville. These storms eventually weakened during the mid evening hours with the passage of the trough through the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down on County Road 452.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed in the south-central Texas Panhandle and central South Plains on the afternoon of the 11th along a diffuse dryline aided by an upper level disturbance. These storms tracked into the Rolling Plains and southeastern Texas Panhandle into the evening with some storms becoming severe. Additional severe thunderstorms developed across the Big Country into the Rolling Plains affecting Stonewall County. Hail as large as golf balls was reported with a storm that moved over Aspermont (Stonewall County) during the afternoon, while penny to quarter size hail was observed near Memphis (Hall County), Childress (Childress County), and Kirkland (Childress County) during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS cooperative weather observer reported golf ball size hail in the town of Aspermont. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough ejected east-northeast across the Oklahoma/Texas Panhandles during the morning of April 22nd, before shifting across much of Oklahoma during the afternoon. Meanwhile, a warm front over Central and Southeast Texas into Southern Louisiana lifted slowly north into portions of East Texas and Central Louisiana during the afternoon. Large scale forcing quickly increased ahead of this trough such that showers and thunderstorms developed near and north of the front during the day, where cold temperatures aloft ahead of the trough and north of the warm front contributed to steep lapse rates, resulting in slightly elevated showers and thunderstorms to develop over much of East Texas and North Louisiana. The storms over Deep East Texas and the southern sections of North Central Louisiana near the front which were rooted closer to the surface became severe, producing more in the way of damaging winds and large hail, with storms farther north across Northwest Louisiana more elevated, producing more sporadic wind damage and large hail. Locally heavy rainfall over relatively saturated grounds also resulted in instances of flash flooding as well. These storms eventually pushed out of the region during the evening hours, with the passage of a weak cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a continuation of the Lamar County EF-2 tornado. As this tornado crossed over into Red River County, it had already weakened to EF-0 strength, with estimated maximum winds near 80 mph. Drone footage indicated that several trees/tree limbs were snapped and uprooted as it entered Red River County, before lifting about one-quarter mile into the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southerly return flow commenced throughout the day on April 24th, in wake of a cold frontal passage that had occurred a couple of days prior on the 22nd. However, low level moisture return was meager, but a sharply amplifying upper level trough over the Central Plains and Midwest region dug south into the Red River Valley of Southern Oklahoma and North Texas during the afternoon. Strong large scale forcing occurred ahead of this trough over Eastern and Southern Oklahoma into Northeast Texas, along and ahead of a reinforcing cold front that shifted southeast over these areas. Strong wind shear also developed ahead of the trough during the afternoon and evening, with very steep lapse rates noted beneath the trough over the Red River Valley into Northeast Texas. This contributed to the development of scattered severe thunderstorms during the afternoon over portions of Southern and Southeast Oklahoma and Northeast Texas, with several supercell thunderstorms developing and tracking southeast over these areas through the evening, and eventually into Northern Louisiana during the mid and late evening hours. Numerous reports of large hail were reported from these storms, with some isolated instances of damaging winds and funnel clouds across portions of Northeast Texas. Multiple cell mergers contributed to an extremely severe supercell with a large swath of destructive straight line winds in excess of 80-90 mph developing over Southern Morris and much of Cass Counties, which accelerated southeast into portions of Northwest Louisiana by mid-evening, thus ending the severe threat for Northeast Texas with the cold frontal passage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell on FM 1402 north of Mount Pleasant.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense surface low pressure center north of the Texas and Oklahoma Panhandles moved from southeast Colorado into southwest Kansas during the late morning and early afternoon. The associated strong pressure gradient across the Panhandles resulted in numerous reports of high winds across the western two-thirds of the Texas Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This gust was measured at the West Texas Mesonet station 1 mile west-northwest of Fritch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extensive squall line developed over the southern plains during the afternoon of the 28th and moved southward through central and east Texas during the night. The squall line reached the Middle Texas coast during the morning hours of the 29th. Wind gusts up to 60 mph occurred over the Coastal Bend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NOS site at Bob Hall Pier measured a gust to 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak shortwave trough shifted east across East Texas and North Louisiana during the morning hours of April 28th, where large scale forcing contributed to the development of showers and thunderstorms over East Texas and North Louisiana. Cold air aloft associated with this trough resulted in steep lapse rates such that isolated severe thunderstorms developed over portions of East Texas throughout the morning and afternoon, where instances of large hail were reported. These storms diminished during the late afternoon, but a deepening upper level trough sweeping southeast across Oklahoma and Arkansas reinforced a cold front southeast across the Southern Plains and Ozarks region, where a complex of showers and thunderstorms developed by mid and late afternoon over these areas and quickly progressed southeast into the Four State Region. Despite the showers and thunderstorms that affected the area earlier in the day, instability remained adequate especially over East Texas, Southeast Oklahoma, and adjacent sections of Southwest Arkansas. As large scale forcing increased ahead of the trough, thus tapping into this unstable air, a squall line progressed southeast through all of the region during the late evening through the early morning hours on April 29th, producing widespread reports of damaging winds and large hail. A couple of isolated tornadoes also touched down across McCurtain County Oklahoma, one associated with the supercell that developed ahead of the line, and another associated within the squall line itself. These storms exited Deep East Texas and the southern sections of Northcentral Louisiana into Southeast Texas and South Louisiana by 4 AM.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A picture of quarter size hail that fell at Lake Brenda just north of Mineola, was posted to social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: April 2020 picked up where March left off - much above average temperatures and dry conditions - which allowed  drought conditions ranging from severe (mainly east of Interstate 69C) to extreme (mainly west of Interstate 69C) to continue to begin the month. On the 3rd and 4th, however, thunderstorms tracked from the ranchlands of northern Zapata into southern Jim Hogg/southern Brooks, then through northern Hidalgo and Willacy County, and improvements would begin to show up by April 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Exceptional (D4) Drought covered all but the northwestern quarter of Zapata County into early April, before torrential rains associated with thunderstorms overnight on April 3rd into the early morning of April 4th dropped between 1.5 and more than 5 inches in a slice of the northern part of the county, bringing a one category improvement in these areas.  Southern Zapata would remain Exceptional a bit longer into April as those rains missed this region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm that edged eastward from the Sierra Madre produced gusty winds in excess of 50 mph and a couple of reports of minor damage to trees, power lines, and a recreational vehicle during the evening of April 28th, 2020.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Deputy Emergency Management Coordinator, along with the public, reported several instances of minor wind damage in Zapata and Siesta Shores just to the south during the mid evening of April 28, 2020. Damage included several felled limbs of mesquite trees in Zapata City park, a poorly constructed awning blown down to an recreational vehicle on the west side of the city, and a power pole leaning along Swope Lane in Siesta Shores. Based on other recent wind events in the City, wind speeds were estimated to be just below severe (58 mph) levels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The southwestern corner of South Central Texas saw another dry month. Dimmit and Maverick Counties remained in Extreme (D3) drought. The northern part of Zavala County had above normal rain, but despite this it only improved from D3 to D2 (Severe). Dimmit and Maverick Counties had burn bans in effect. Larger public water systems in Zavala County had voluntary water restrictions in place.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, long-lasting supercell that developed across the southern portion of Jim Hogg county during the predawn hours of April 4th along with scattered showers brought some relief to drought stricken areas across the upper Rio Grande Valley and the northern ranchlands. Pasture and rangeland conditions were in poor condition in most areas but started to improve in areas with recent rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A long-lasting, strong supercell moved through Willacy County on the morning of April 4th, bringing much needed rainfall along with some hail and damaging winds. Rainfall was sufficient to drop the Severe (D2) Drought conditions across the northern half of the coastal sections, leaving only the bottom half in Severe (D2) Drought through the end of the month into May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, long-lasting supercell that developed across the southern portion of Jim Hogg county during the predawn hours of April 4th along with scattered showers brought some relief to drought stricken areas across the upper Rio Grande Valley and the northern ranchlands. Pasture and rangeland conditions were in poor condition in most areas but started to improve in areas with recent rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall from a fast-moving supercell thunderstorm in the southern half of coastal Kenedy County dropped 1.5 to 2 inches of rainfall early on April 4th, improving drought from Severe (D2) to Moderate (D1) while leaving the northern half of the coastline unchanged.  These conditions would remain through the remainder of April.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, long-lasting supercell that developed across the southern portion of Jim Hogg county during the predawn hours of April 4th along with scattered showers brought some relief to drought stricken areas across the upper Rio Grande Valley and the northern ranchlands. Pasture and rangeland conditions were in poor condition in most areas but started to improve in areas with recent rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall mostly associated with the supercell event during the morning of April 4th brought some minor drought relief to Zapata County at the beginning of April. However, the bottom third of the county remained in Exceptional (D4) Drought, with the middle third in Extreme (D3) Drought, and the top third in Severe (D2) Drought starting on April 7th. Zapata County reported poor pasture and rangeland conditions at the beginning of April, with some bare pastures. Producers in Zapata continued to sell off cattle or feed hay and cubes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level shortwave trough brought a cold front through South Central Texas. The front moved into a warm, moist airmass and generated thunderstorms. Some of these storms produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning caused a natural gas explosion near the intersection of Hwy 87 and Loop 1604 in eastern Bexar County. The explosion was 200 yards by 200 yards. Evacuations were ordered within two miles of the blast and a shelter in place order within eight miles. There were no injuries, fatalities, or structures damaged.

Multiple cell mergers contributed to an extremely severe supercell with a large swath of destructive straight line winds in excess of 80-90 mph developing over Southern Morris and much of Cass Counties, which accelerated southeast into portions of Northwest Louisiana by mid-evening, thus ending the severe threat for Northeast Texas with the cold frontal passage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A photo of half dollar size hail that fell in the New Hope community just south of Bloomburg was posted to social media.

An automobile service building on Houston Street in downtown Linden had its metal awning destroyed. Another building at the corner of South Church Street and Houston Street had a portion of its metal roof ripped off, bringing down cinder blocks from a wall and destroying a vehicle parked nearby. Locations along Texas Street, Oklahoma Street, Kildare Road, and Arkansas Avenue were particularly hard|hit with a more concentrated area of straight-line winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much needed rainfall fell across areas where exceptional drought had persisted (mostly in southern Zapata County) during mid April. Unfortunately, most of the remainder of Deep South Texas has received less than one quarter of an inch through the same time period, especially across the Rio Grande Valley, where April temperatures remained well above normal. Overall average temperatures through April ranked between the third to fifth warmest in recorded history between Brownsville, Harlingen, and McAllen, after temperatures in March notched the record warmest at all three locations. For all of April up until this point...the majority of rainfall has fallen across the northern ranchlands of Deep South Texas and the northern edge of the Rio Grande Valley. These areas received anywhere between generally 50 to 110 percent of their normal rainfall. The rest of Deep South Texas, including most of the Rio Grande Valley, have received between 5 to 50 percent of their normal rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall during mid April was sufficient to improve drought conditions in Zapata County, dropping the area of Exceptional (D4) Drought to Extreme (D3) Drought starting April 21st and continuing through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense surface low pressure center north of the Texas and Oklahoma Panhandles moved from southeast Colorado into southwest Kansas during the late morning and early afternoon. The associated strong pressure gradient across the Panhandles resulted in numerous reports of high winds across the western two-thirds of the Texas Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Texas Mesonet station 15 miles north-northwest of Amarillo measured sustained winds in the 40 to 44 MPH range during this time period. The highest sustained wind of 44 MPH occurred at 15:37.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm, moist, and unstable air surged north into East Texas, North Louisiana, and Southern Arkansas during the evening through the early morning hours of April 11th-12th, along a warm front that lifted north across much of East Texas into North Louisiana. Meanwhile, a strong upper level low opened up into a trough as it entered the Southern Plains during the evening of the 11th, with large scale forcing ahead of the trough enhancing shower and thunderstorm development across much of West-central, Central, East and Southeast Texas during the evening through the early morning hours. Given the extent of shear, instability, and upper level forcing in place across the warm sector, numerous showers and thunderstorms developed over these areas, which quickly spread east northeast into East Texas around and shortly after daybreak on the 12th. Some of these storms were severe, producing numerous reports of damaging winds, large hail, and locally heavy rainfall which resulted in isolated flash flooding. Two isolated tornadoes also touched down in Shelby and Southern Harrison Counties. ||While there was a lull in the thunderstorms from late morning through much of the afternoon, additional strong to severe thunderstorms developed across portions of Northeast Texas well behind the dry line which had mixed east to near Texarkana into Western Louisiana, and near the center of the upper level trough. Steep lapse rates aloft and the drier low level air mass contributed to areas of damaging winds and isolated instances of large hail, with the majority of the wind damage from a collapsing thunderstorm which originated just east of Dallas, and quickly moved east along the I-20 corridor in East Texas. Aided by strong pressure rises behind an associated cold front, a large swath of damaging winds were noted with the severe gust front, with gusts ranging from 50-70 mph well ahead of the collapsed storm. This resulted in numerous reports of downed trees and power lines across East Texas and Western Louisiana, with AEP Swepco noting that over 64,000 customers were without power at one time in the aftermath of the storms. These damaging winds exited East Texas into Western Louisiana by early evening before gradually weakening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A home was struck by lightning in Flint. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon in a moist and unstable airmass along and east of a stalled northeast-to-southwest-oriented dryline. Severe hail was reported from one thunderstorm in the southeast Texas Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch hail was reported between County Roads X and Y on U.S. Highway 83.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several severe thunderstorms developed across the Permian Basin on the afternoon of the 10th. A few of these storms raced northeastward into the southeast South Plains and western Rolling Plains before falling apart. One of the storms did drop nickel size hail seven miles southeast of Post along US Highway 84 before rapidly weakening.

Scattered thunderstorms developed in the south-central Texas Panhandle and central South Plains on the afternoon of the 11th along a diffuse dryline aided by an upper level disturbance. These storms tracked into the Rolling Plains and southeastern Texas Panhandle into the evening with some storms becoming severe. Additional severe thunderstorms developed across the Big Country into the Rolling Plains affecting Stonewall County. Hail as large as golf balls was reported with a storm that moved over Aspermont (Stonewall County) during the afternoon, while penny to quarter size hail was observed near Memphis (Hall County), Childress (Childress County), and Kirkland (Childress County) during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report was received of half dollar size hail approximately five miles south of Peacock.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High-based thunderstorms developed across the west-central Texas Panhandle during the afternoon. As these thunderstorms moved east, they crossed a north-south oriented dryline, with cloud bases lowering considerably. Additionally, with winds out of the east present on the eastern side of the dryline, these storms moved into an environment with low-level shear profiles conducive for the development of tornadoes. Furthermore, the presence of the dryline allowed for the potential development of landspout tornadoes for any storms that were located directly on or very close to the dryline. Two tornadoes did occur,  one in Carson County and a landspout tornado in Sherman County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado remained in open country grasslands, touching down approximately             10 miles north of Pantex, and lifting approximately 11 miles north northeast of Pantex. The tornado did no visible damage. Path details were estimated based on chaser|pictures/reports and radar. Since this tornado remained in the open country grasslands and did no visible damage, it was given a rating of EF-Unknown as well as an unknown estimated peak wind speed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High-based thunderstorms developed across the west-central Texas Panhandle during the afternoon. As these thunderstorms moved east, they crossed a north-south oriented dryline, with cloud bases lowering considerably. Additionally, with winds out of the east present on the eastern side of the dryline, these storms moved into an environment with low-level shear profiles conducive for the development of tornadoes. Furthermore, the presence of the dryline allowed for the potential development of landspout tornadoes for any storms that were located directly on or very close to the dryline. Two tornadoes did occur,  one in Carson County and a landspout tornado in Sherman County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A landspout tornado touched down approximately 10 miles north of Sunray. The tornado moved very little, remained in a field and did no damage, before lifting approximately 10 miles north of Sunray. This tornado was rated EF-0 with an estimated peak wind speed of 75 MPH. Location and path details are estimated based on photographs from a local storm spotter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm, moist, and unstable air surged north into East Texas, North Louisiana, and Southern Arkansas during the evening through the early morning hours of April 11th-12th, along a warm front that lifted north across much of East Texas into North Louisiana. Meanwhile, a strong upper level low opened up into a trough as it entered the Southern Plains during the evening of the 11th, with large scale forcing ahead of the trough enhancing shower and thunderstorm development across much of West-central, Central, East and Southeast Texas during the evening through the early morning hours. Given the extent of shear, instability, and upper level forcing in place across the warm sector, numerous showers and thunderstorms developed over these areas, which quickly spread east northeast into East Texas around and shortly after daybreak on the 12th. Some of these storms were severe, producing numerous reports of damaging winds, large hail, and locally heavy rainfall which resulted in isolated flash flooding. Two isolated tornadoes also touched down in Shelby and Southern Harrison Counties. ||While there was a lull in the thunderstorms from late morning through much of the afternoon, additional strong to severe thunderstorms developed across portions of Northeast Texas well behind the dry line which had mixed east to near Texarkana into Western Louisiana, and near the center of the upper level trough. Steep lapse rates aloft and the drier low level air mass contributed to areas of damaging winds and isolated instances of large hail, with the majority of the wind damage from a collapsing thunderstorm which originated just east of Dallas, and quickly moved east along the I-20 corridor in East Texas. Aided by strong pressure rises behind an associated cold front, a large swath of damaging winds were noted with the severe gust front, with gusts ranging from 50-70 mph well ahead of the collapsed storm. This resulted in numerous reports of downed trees and power lines across East Texas and Western Louisiana, with AEP Swepco noting that over 64,000 customers were without power at one time in the aftermath of the storms. These damaging winds exited East Texas into Western Louisiana by early evening before gradually weakening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of Highway 80 were temporarily closed due to flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the overnight into the morning of January 11, snow fell across the Big Country, mainly the region along and north of Interstate 20.  This area saw about 1 to 3 inches.  As temperatures fell behind a cold front, an upper level low tracking across the area brought this blanket of snow.  There were not any major problems reported on area roads because temperatures were near freezing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High Resolution Visible Satellite data showed at least a dusting of snow across eastern Fisher County.

As temperatures fell behind a cold front, an upper level low tracking across the area brought this blanket of snow. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement officials from Haskell County Sheriff's Office estimated about a half inch of snow fell in Haskell.  High Resolution Visible Satellite data showed most of the snow across the southeastern half of Haskell County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north into Deep East Texas and the southern sections of North Central Louisiana by afternoon on April 19th, ahead of a vigorous upper level trough that ejected east from New Mexico across Texas. Large scale forcing increased by afternoon ahead of the trough as it approached the expanding warm sector, characteristic of moderate instability and strong wind shear. Areas of showers and thunderstorms developed and became widespread near and north of the warm front, and limited the northward progress of the front, but severe thunderstorms still developed within the warm sector across Deep East Texas and Northcentral Louisiana, resulting in areas of damaging winds across these areas. An isolated elevated severe thunderstorm also developed over portions of Lamar County Texas, which moved into Red River County and produced quarter size hail near Clarksville. These storms eventually weakened during the mid evening hours with the passage of the trough through the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping pong ball size hail fell in the Lilbert community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level trough and a humid and unstable environment resulted in an early severe weather event across North and Central Texas. Severe storms developed in the afternoon hours to the northwest and north of the DFW Metroplex. These storms eventually developed into an expanding squall line. A total of 5 tornadoes occurred with this event along with some damaging wind and smaller-sized hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moved quickly over southern Wise County during the mid-afternoon hours on Friday, January 10th.  This thunderstorm spawned a tornado approximately 4.3 miles WNW of Boyd, just off of County Road 4598.  This tornado destroyed a barn and deposited the debris in a path roughly a quarter mile downstream from the barn's location.  Some light damage to a nearby gas well, and some isolated large tree limbs damage also occurred. The tornado was on the ground for approximately 1 minute before dissipating.  This tornado was rated an EF-0 with maximum winds of 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level low emerged across the southern High Plains on the 28th which brought minor snow accumulations to the southeastern Texas Panhandle. One heavy band of precipitation developed across the southeastern Texas Panhandle into the northern Rolling Plains during the morning hours. This band lingered for several hours before finally weakening and shifting eastward in the afternoon. The heaviest snow fell in a small corridor along and near US Highway 287 from Memphis (Hall County) to west of Childress (Childress County), where one to three inches of snow was reported.||A NWS cooperative weather observer in Memphis (Hall County) reported 3.0 inches of snowfall. An observer in Tell (Childress County) reported 2.0 inches of snowfall.

An upper level disturbance produced scattered showers and isolated thunderstorms along and north of a stalling front over Central Texas. Locally heavy rain brought isolated flash flooding in the city of Fort Worth, TX.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fire and rescue reported a high water rescue near the interchange of East Rosedale Street and East Loop 820 in the city of Fort Worth, TX.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the overnight into the morning of January 11, snow fell across the Big Country, mainly the region along and north of Interstate 20.  This area saw about 1 to 3 inches.  As temperatures fell behind a cold front, an upper level low tracking across the area brought this blanket of snow.  There were not any major problems reported on area roads because temperatures were near freezing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Jones County Sheriff's office estimated 2 inches of snow fell in Anson.  High Resolution Visible Satellite data showed a large area of snow across the northern half of Jones County, including the town of Anson.  According to satellite data, patchy areas of snow could be seen in the southern half of Jones County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The same supercell that produced the earlier Cooper tornado moved northeast about 4 miles and spawned a second very brief tornado near Enloe, in northern Delta County. The tornado touched down in an open field just southeast of Enloe, destroying a couple of small outbuildings and scattering the debris downwind a few hundred yards. The debris pattern exhibited a subtle cyclonic orientation, implying a brief, weak tornado. The tornado was rated an EF-0 with maximum winds of 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level trough and a humid and unstable environment resulted in an early severe weather event across North and Central Texas. Severe storms developed in the afternoon hours to the northwest and north of the DFW Metroplex. These storms eventually developed into an expanding squall line. A total of 5 tornadoes occurred with this event along with some damaging wind and smaller-sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado began at approximately 5:41 PM CST in an open field west of CR-913A and damaged several homes and buildings near Piester and CR-913A. A barn was flipped and came to rest on the side of a house. Several homes in the area had damaged roofs and/or collapsed attached awnings. A large horse trailer was moved and an 18 wheeler trailer was overturned. A small 5 ft by 6 ft storage shed was damaged as well as 4 power poles leaning 10-15 degrees. Just northeast of this area, several homes near the end of Dove Meadow Lane sustained minor damage. Shingles were removed from homes and sheds were damaged. The tornado dissipated before reaching FM 1014. This tornado was rated an EF-1 with maximum winds of 90 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak shortwave trough shifted east across East Texas and North Louisiana during the morning hours of April 28th, where large scale forcing contributed to the development of showers and thunderstorms over East Texas and North Louisiana. Cold air aloft associated with this trough resulted in steep lapse rates such that isolated severe thunderstorms developed over portions of East Texas throughout the morning and afternoon, where instances of large hail were reported. These storms diminished during the late afternoon, but a deepening upper level trough sweeping southeast across Oklahoma and Arkansas reinforced a cold front southeast across the Southern Plains and Ozarks region, where a complex of showers and thunderstorms developed by mid and late afternoon over these areas and quickly progressed southeast into the Four State Region. Despite the showers and thunderstorms that affected the area earlier in the day, instability remained adequate especially over East Texas, Southeast Oklahoma, and adjacent sections of Southwest Arkansas. As large scale forcing increased ahead of the trough, thus tapping into this unstable air, a squall line progressed southeast through all of the region during the late evening through the early morning hours on April 29th, producing widespread reports of damaging winds and large hail. A couple of isolated tornadoes also touched down across McCurtain County Oklahoma, one associated with the supercell that developed ahead of the line, and another associated within the squall line itself. These storms exited Deep East Texas and the southern sections of Northcentral Louisiana into Southeast Texas and South Louisiana by 4 AM.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down in Hughes Springs. Report from mPING.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level trough and a humid and unstable environment resulted in an early severe weather event across North and Central Texas. Severe storms developed in the afternoon hours to the northwest and north of the DFW Metroplex. These storms eventually developed into an expanding squall line. A total of 5 tornadoes occurred with this event along with some damaging wind and smaller-sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small supercell thunderstorm moved northeast into Delta County, Texas, spawning a brief tornado on the west side of the City of Cooper. The tornado formed about two blocks south of West Dallas Avenue in west Cooper, destroying a small outbuilding and causing minor roof and tree damage. The tornado moved northeast, crossing Dallas Avenue and pulling the roof and an exterior cinder block wall off of a small commercial building. Exterior siding damage also occurred to a two-story apartment complex nearby. The tornado continued toward the northeast, seriously damaging a large sheet metal cotton gin building. Minor tree and structural damage continued for approximately another 200 yards before the tornado dissipated south of State Highway 24. This tornado was rated an EF-0 with maximum winds of 85 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado formed along the eastern edge of the Hackberry Creek neighborhood, near the intersection of Kinwest Parkway and Parkridge Boulevard. Minor roof and tree damage was observed in this area. The tornado moved quickly toward the northeast, producing roof damage to a grocery store and nearby trees, lightpoles, and commercial signage near the intersection of North MacArthur Boulevard and Las Colinas Boulevard. Continuing northeast, exterior wall damage occurred at a nearby hotel, and window and roofing damage impacted two restaurants fronting Interstate 635. The tornado crossed the Interstate and produced minor tree and roof damage before dissipating near Ranchview Drive. This tornado was rated an EF-0 with maximum winds of 75 mph.

Cold air aloft associated with this trough resulted in steep lapse rates such that isolated severe thunderstorms developed over portions of East Texas throughout the morning and afternoon, where instances of large hail were reported. These storms diminished during the late afternoon, but a deepening upper level trough sweeping southeast across Oklahoma and Arkansas reinforced a cold front southeast across the Southern Plains and Ozarks region, where a complex of showers and thunderstorms developed by mid and late afternoon over these areas and quickly progressed southeast into the Four State Region. Despite the showers and thunderstorms that affected the area earlier in the day, instability remained adequate especially over East Texas, Southeast Oklahoma, and adjacent sections of Southwest Arkansas. As large scale forcing increased ahead of the trough, thus tapping into this unstable air, a squall line progressed southeast through all of the region during the late evening through the early morning hours on April 29th, producing widespread reports of damaging winds and large hail. A couple of isolated tornadoes also touched down across McCurtain County Oklahoma, one associated with the supercell that developed ahead of the line, and another associated within the squall line itself. These storms exited Deep East Texas and the southern sections of Northcentral Louisiana into Southeast Texas and South Louisiana by 4 AM.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed across FM 10 in Carthage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level trough and a humid and unstable environment resulted in an early severe weather event across North and Central Texas. Severe storms developed in the afternoon hours to the northwest and north of the DFW Metroplex. These storms eventually developed into an expanding squall line. A total of 5 tornadoes occurred with this event along with some damaging wind and smaller-sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roof damage was reported in Palestine and power poles were also damaged. The report from a member of the nearby NASA balloon facility wrote: a microburst occurred in Palestine near the Courthouse by the intersection of Church and Poplar Street. The power poles that snapped were blown in one direction. Palestine Transmission Service suffered roof damage, and a metal building including beams was destroyed and blown across the street. There was no other damage in the immediate area..

A deep upper trough produced a round of snow across parts of North Texas as it moved overhead during the early morning hours of Saturday, January 11. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amateur radio reported 3 inches of snow at Hubbard Creek Lake, or approximately 2 miles west of the city of Breckenridge, TX.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north into Deep East Texas and the southern sections of North Central Louisiana by afternoon on April 19th, ahead of a vigorous upper level trough that ejected east from New Mexico across Texas. Large scale forcing increased by afternoon ahead of the trough as it approached the expanding warm sector, characteristic of moderate instability and strong wind shear. Areas of showers and thunderstorms developed and became widespread near and north of the warm front, and limited the northward progress of the front, but severe thunderstorms still developed within the warm sector across Deep East Texas and Northcentral Louisiana, resulting in areas of damaging winds across these areas. An isolated elevated severe thunderstorm also developed over portions of Lamar County Texas, which moved into Red River County and produced quarter size hail near Clarksville. These storms eventually weakened during the mid evening hours with the passage of the trough through the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail fell along Highway 259 northwest of Appleby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A landspout touched down about a mile west of Ross and tracked into Ross. It damaged 2 metal roofs and flipped a boat and was rated an EF-0.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A landspout tornado flipped a boat and damaged roof panels at a property on Fort Graham Road to the west of Ross, and then traveled (likely non-continuously) into Ross where it damaged the metal roof of a carport at a store in town. Video and picture evidence confirmed this small landspout.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of intermittent light freezing rain and freezing drizzle across the Texas Panhandle resulted in ice accumulations as high as 0.20 inch.  Icy roads were reported across all counties of the Texas Panhandle.  A few reports of traffic accidents were received from the northeast and north-central Texas Panhandle, namely at Canadian in Hemphill County and near Spearman in Hansford County.  One accident in Amarillo resulted in death and injury.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain caused some areas of ice on some streets and roadways. In Amarillo, around 08:22, the ice on a bridge and speed led to a driver losing control of her vehicle, which slid into oncoming traffic, colliding into another car. The driver of the first car died at the scene of the accident. The driver of the second vehicle was taken to a local hospital with what was initially believed to be life-threatening injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough dug into northern Mexico before moving east into the South Plains. Strong winds developed in the Guadalupe and Davis Mountains as the trough moved across the area.
An upper level trough dug into northern Mexico before moving east into the South Plains. Strong winds developed in the Guadalupe and Davis Mountains as the trough moved across the area.

An upper level trough shifted east across the Four State Region during the afternoon and evening hours of April 9th, as it eventually became absorbed into northwest flow aloft as a longwave trough dug through the Plains and Mississippi Valley. This trough allowed for a cold front to slowly backdoor southwest into East Texas and North Louisiana, before becoming stationary over portions of Deep East Texas across the Toledo Bend Reservoir into Central Louisiana. Strong afternoon heating and rich low level moisture south of the front provided for moderate instability during the mid and late afternoon hours, with large scale forcing ahead of the approaching trough resulting in scattered showers and thunderstorms increasing by late afternoon near and just south of the front. Some of the storms quickly became severe over portions of Deep East Texas, with multiple reports of large hail over Western and Central Sabine County. These storms tracked southeast into Northern Jasper and Newton Counties, and eventually into Northern Vernon Parishes, where they remained severe as they moved into Southwest Louisiana through the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell in Bronson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific cold front moved into a warm, moist airmass and generated thunderstorms in the warm sector. Some of these storms produced large hail and damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced a measured wind gust of 57 kts. at Kelly Field in San Antonio.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific cold front moved into a warm, moist airmass and generated thunderstorms in the warm sector. Some of these storms produced large hail and damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced a measured wind gust of 67 kts. at Stinson Municipal Airport in San Antonio.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Additional feeder bands redeveloped in unstable air Monday afternoon and evening in western Cameron and eastern Hidalgo County even after Hurricane Hanna moved inland and dissipated across Mexico, exacerbating the ongoing flood conditions in the Rio Grande Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A second round of rainfall dropped an estimated 2 to 5 inches of rain in about two hours between la Feria and Santa Rosa, exacerbating ongoing flooding in western Cameron County and leaving pockets of 2 to 4 feet of water in roads, neighborhoods, and ditches.  Public assistance property damage is listed in the episode for Hurricane Hanna, as it was the trigger event for the continuation of the flooding. Crop damage (Cotton, other dryland crops) due to the heavy rain is listed in the episode for Hurricane Hanna.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough ejected east-northeast across the Oklahoma/Texas Panhandles during the morning of April 22nd, before shifting across much of Oklahoma during the afternoon. Meanwhile, a warm front over Central and Southeast Texas into Southern Louisiana lifted slowly north into portions of East Texas and Central Louisiana during the afternoon. Large scale forcing quickly increased ahead of this trough such that showers and thunderstorms developed near and north of the front during the day, where cold temperatures aloft ahead of the trough and north of the warm front contributed to steep lapse rates, resulting in slightly elevated showers and thunderstorms to develop over much of East Texas and North Louisiana. The storms over Deep East Texas and the southern sections of North Central Louisiana near the front which were rooted closer to the surface became severe, producing more in the way of damaging winds and large hail, with storms farther north across Northwest Louisiana more elevated, producing more sporadic wind damage and large hail. Locally heavy rainfall over relatively saturated grounds also resulted in instances of flash flooding as well. These storms eventually pushed out of the region during the evening hours, with the passage of a weak cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down across Highway 42.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe to extreme drought conditions existed across all of south Texas at the beginning of the month. Above normal rainfall occurred during the first half of the month over the Brush Country. Drought conditions improved during the month with severe drought continuing over the coastal plains by the end of the month. There were some isolated pockets of extreme drought lingering over the Victoria Crossroads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought continued over the area through the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front moved south through the Ark-La-Tex to the Texas and Louisiana coasts on the morning of April 18th, although this front was shallow with its remnant elevated front stalling along the I-20 corridor of East Texas and North Louisiana. Elevated moisture return commenced during the afternoon hours atop this shallow post-frontal air mass, as weak shortwave energy ejected east northeast over the region ahead of an upper trough over the Four Corners Region. Ample instability aloft remained ahead of this approaching disturbance, with isolated to widely scattered showers and thunderstorms developing during the late afternoon and early evening hours. One of these storms briefly became severe, producing quarter size hail north of Cushing in Southern Rusk County. Additional scattered showers and thunderstorms developed overnight ahead of a northward moving warm front, but these remained sub-severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel to quarter size hail fell at a Plant Nursery north of Cushing off of Highway 84.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north into Deep East Texas and the southern sections of North Central Louisiana by afternoon on April 19th, ahead of a vigorous upper level trough that ejected east from New Mexico across Texas. Large scale forcing increased by afternoon ahead of the trough as it approached the expanding warm sector, characteristic of moderate instability and strong wind shear. Areas of showers and thunderstorms developed and became widespread near and north of the warm front, and limited the northward progress of the front, but severe thunderstorms still developed within the warm sector across Deep East Texas and Northcentral Louisiana, resulting in areas of damaging winds across these areas. An isolated elevated severe thunderstorm also developed over portions of Lamar County Texas, which moved into Red River County and produced quarter size hail near Clarksville. These storms eventually weakened during the mid evening hours with the passage of the trough through the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down on FM 1648.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough ejected east-northeast across the Oklahoma/Texas Panhandles during the morning of April 22nd, before shifting across much of Oklahoma during the afternoon. Meanwhile, a warm front over Central and Southeast Texas into Southern Louisiana lifted slowly north into portions of East Texas and Central Louisiana during the afternoon. Large scale forcing quickly increased ahead of this trough such that showers and thunderstorms developed near and north of the front during the day, where cold temperatures aloft ahead of the trough and north of the warm front contributed to steep lapse rates, resulting in slightly elevated showers and thunderstorms to develop over much of East Texas and North Louisiana. The storms over Deep East Texas and the southern sections of North Central Louisiana near the front which were rooted closer to the surface became severe, producing more in the way of damaging winds and large hail, with storms farther north across Northwest Louisiana more elevated, producing more sporadic wind damage and large hail. Locally heavy rainfall over relatively saturated grounds also resulted in instances of flash flooding as well. These storms eventually pushed out of the region during the evening hours, with the passage of a weak cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on County Road 4945.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions developed briefly during the middle portion of January across much of Harrison and Marion Counties in East Texas, in wake of a very dry November-December 2019 period that was observed across these areas. In fact, the prolonged dryness resulted in area creeks and streams experiencing below normal streamflows through the first half of the month. However, the frequency of rainfall events increased across East Texas during the middle and latter half of January, with monthly rainfall amounts ranging between five and six inches, which was one to three inches above normal. This rain and the resultant runoff it produced, resulted in a one category drought improvement to Moderate Drought (D1) across Marion and much of Harrison Counties for the latter half of January.

Drought stress on ranchlands in Starr, Jim Hogg and Zapata County was maintained with minimal rainfall and mild to warm temperatures; Severe (D2) Drought areal coverage did not change significantly as rainfall favored the coastal counties of the Rio Grande Valley and King Ranch, where rainfall was generally 25 to 50 percent of average.  Extreme Drought (D3) developed across the western portion of Zapata County as drought conditions intensified on January 21st, and monthly rainfall was 10 to 25 percent of average. These conditions were maintained through the end of January with slight intensification into early February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought persisted across the central portion of Starr County on January 21st and then expanded slightly into early February.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific cold front moved into a warm, moist airmass and generated thunderstorms in the warm sector. Some of these storms produced large hail and damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced wind gusts estimated at 75 mph that blew the siding off a house and flipped over a storage pod in Kyle.  There were multiple trees and fences blown down near this neighborhood as well as areas just to the west on the west side of Interstate 35.  Multiple houses had shingle damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe to extreme drought conditions existed across all of south Texas at the beginning of the month. Above normal rainfall occurred during the first half of the month over the Brush Country. Drought conditions improved during the month with severe drought continuing over the coastal plains by the end of the month. There were some isolated pockets of extreme drought lingering over the Victoria Crossroads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought continued over the area through the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level trough and a humid and unstable environment resulted in an early severe weather event across North and Central Texas. Severe storms developed in the afternoon hours to the northwest and north of the DFW Metroplex. These storms eventually developed into an expanding squall line. A total of 5 tornadoes occurred with this event along with some damaging wind and smaller-sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported at Moss Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There were three precipitation regimes over South Central Texas in January. The area east of Hwy 281 received between 75% and 125% of normal. The area west of Hwy 281 and north of Hwy 90 received between 125% and 300% or normal. The area west of Hwy 281 and south of Hwy 90 received less than 50%. This led to some improvement and some worsening of drought conditions. Maverick County went from Severe (D2) Drought to Extreme (D3) Drought. Atascosa, Caldwell, Comal, De Witt, Dimmit, Frio, Gonzales, Guadalupe, Hays, Williamson, and Wilson Counties moved from no drought to Severe (D2). Kinney, Llano, and Uvalde Counties improved from D3 to D2. Zavala stayed in D3 and Blanco, Burnet, Karnes, and Val Verde stayed in D2. Of the counties in D2 or worse drought Atascosa, Dimmit, Frio, Karnes, Kinney, Maverick, Uvalde, Val Verde, and Zavala had burn bans in place. Larger public water systems in Blanco, Burnet, Karnes, and Llano Counties were in Stage 1 water restrictions and in Val Verde and Zavala there were voluntary restrictions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north into Deep East Texas and the southern sections of North Central Louisiana by afternoon on April 19th, ahead of a vigorous upper level trough that ejected east from New Mexico across Texas. Large scale forcing increased by afternoon ahead of the trough as it approached the expanding warm sector, characteristic of moderate instability and strong wind shear. Areas of showers and thunderstorms developed and became widespread near and north of the warm front, and limited the northward progress of the front, but severe thunderstorms still developed within the warm sector across Deep East Texas and Northcentral Louisiana, resulting in areas of damaging winds across these areas. An isolated elevated severe thunderstorm also developed over portions of Lamar County Texas, which moved into Red River County and produced quarter size hail near Clarksville. These storms eventually weakened during the mid evening hours with the passage of the trough through the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to quarter size hail fell in Clarksville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough was centered over southern Arizona. A dryline developed across West Texas. Very moist and unstable air was present to the east of the dryline as well as high wind shear. Upper lift increased as the upper trough moved closer to the region. These conditions resulted in thunderstorms that produced large hail across the Permian Basin.

An upper level trough was centered over southern Arizona. A dryline developed across West Texas. Very moist and unstable air was present to the east of the dryline as well as high wind shear. Upper lift increased as the upper trough moved closer to the region. These conditions resulted in thunderstorms that produced large hail across the Permian Basin.

A weak shortwave trough shifted east across East Texas and North Louisiana during the morning hours of April 28th, where large scale forcing contributed to the development of showers and thunderstorms over East Texas and North Louisiana. Cold air aloft associated with this trough resulted in steep lapse rates such that isolated severe thunderstorms developed over portions of East Texas throughout the morning and afternoon, where instances of large hail were reported. These storms diminished during the late afternoon, but a deepening upper level trough sweeping southeast across Oklahoma and Arkansas reinforced a cold front southeast across the Southern Plains and Ozarks region, where a complex of showers and thunderstorms developed by mid and late afternoon over these areas and quickly progressed southeast into the Four State Region. Despite the showers and thunderstorms that affected the area earlier in the day, instability remained adequate especially over East Texas, Southeast Oklahoma, and adjacent sections of Southwest Arkansas. As large scale forcing increased ahead of the trough, thus tapping into this unstable air, a squall line progressed southeast through all of the region during the late evening through the early morning hours on April 29th, producing widespread reports of damaging winds and large hail. A couple of isolated tornadoes also touched down across McCurtain County Oklahoma, one associated with the supercell that developed ahead of the line, and another associated within the squall line itself. These storms exited Deep East Texas and the southern sections of Northcentral Louisiana into Southeast Texas and South Louisiana by 4 AM.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell near Clark Taylor Road in Hallsville.

As large scale forcing increased ahead of the trough, thus tapping into this unstable air, a squall line progressed southeast through all of the region during the late evening through the early morning hours on April 29th, producing widespread reports of damaging winds and large hail. A couple of isolated tornadoes also touched down across McCurtain County Oklahoma, one associated with the supercell that developed ahead of the line, and another associated within the squall line itself. These storms exited Deep East Texas and the southern sections of Northcentral Louisiana into Southeast Texas and South Louisiana by 4 AM.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed on FM 2625 west near 5 Notch Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak shortwave trough shifted east across East Texas and North Louisiana during the morning hours of April 28th, where large scale forcing contributed to the development of showers and thunderstorms over East Texas and North Louisiana. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions developed throughout much of the month of January across Rusk and Gregg Counties in East Texas, in wake of a very dry November-December 2019 period that was observed across these areas. In fact, the prolonged dryness resulted in the Sabine River basin (including portions of these two counties) experiencing below normal streamflows through much of the month. However, the frequency of rainfall events increased across East Texas during the middle and latter half of January, with monthly rainfall amounts ranging between four and five inches, which was one to two inches above normal. This rain, the resultant runoff it produced, and the improved streamflows across the Sabine River Basin, resulted in a one category drought improvement to Moderate Drought (D1) across much of Rusk and Gregg Counties to end the month.

A potent upper level low emerged across the southern High Plains on the 28th which brought minor snow accumulations to the southeastern Texas Panhandle. One heavy band of precipitation developed across the southeastern Texas Panhandle into the northern Rolling Plains during the morning hours. This band lingered for several hours before finally weakening and shifting eastward in the afternoon. The heaviest snow fell in a small corridor along and near US Highway 287 from Memphis (Hall County) to west of Childress (Childress County), where one to three inches of snow was reported.||A NWS cooperative weather observer in Memphis (Hall County) reported 3.0 inches of snowfall. An observer in Tell (Childress County) reported 2.0 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level shortwave trough moved around a low over the southwestern US and enhanced lift over the mountains in northern Mexico and a dryline in West Texas. These features interacted with a warm, moist airmass over South Central Texas to generate thunderstorms. Some of these storms produced tornadoes and large hail. The large hail that hit the City of Del Rio and near Brackettville likely caused in excess of 10 million dollars in damage but as of this writing do not have an accurate estimate of damage from city/county/state officials or private insurance companies.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced softball size hail in Brackettville. This report was from a picture on social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm which developed over southwest Oklahoma along a dryline tracked southeastward through the northwest counties of North Texas Thursday evening. The storm produced a path of hail from north of Jacksboro to the far western portions of the DFW Metroplex. Additional thunderstorms developed overnight as a cold front pushed south of the Red River, but most of these storms remained below severe criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amateur radio reported half-dollar sized hail near the Interstate 20 and Highway 180 split in the city of Hudson Oaks, TX.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: May 22nd - 24th, 2020 was an active few days with two main time frames of severe weather, and scattered severe weather reports punctuated elsewhere within these few days. The highlight of this period was damaging straight-line winds across Montague, Cooke and Grayson Counties with an EF-1 tornado in Bowie (Montague County) on May 22nd. These storms formed along an outflow boundary and front combined across our northwestern counties. The second significant round of convection was scattered diurnal storms on the 24th that produced a few instances of large hail. This second event occurred ahead of an upper level trough that set the stage for severe weather in the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This event continued into Grayson County. Strong thunderstorm straight-line winds produced tree and light structural damage across a 12 mile path in eastern Cooke and extreme western Grayson Counties. Scattered snapped and uprooted trees were observed generally along US Highway 82, from approximately 2 miles east of the center of Gainesville, to just east of Whitesboro. North of Highway 82 near the Oak Ridge area, a few RVs were damaged. Continuing east from there, the hardest hit area was east of FM 678 where roofs were partially torn off a few homes and off a large ranch arena.||The damage path varied from 1 to 2 miles in width. All of the tree damage was blown in an east-southeast direction, and nearly all shingle damage was confined to the west or upwind sides of the structures. These factors are suggestive of a sustained straight-line downburst wind event.

With April temperatures remaining well above normal and limited rainfall the last week of April, Severe (D2) to Extreme (D3) Drought persisted into the second week of May. Extreme (D3) drought conditions continued across southern Zapata, western Starr, southeastern Jim Hogg, along the Rio Grande in southern Starr County, and across southeastern Cameron County. Severe (D2) drought conditions continued across central Zapata, southwestern Jim Hogg, northwestern Brooks, northern Kenedy, south and southwestern Willacy, the remainder of Cameron, and majority of the remainder of both Starr and Hidalgo counties. These drought conditions affected agricultural interests and grasslands as well as hydrologic and ecologic interests across most of Deep South Texas and the Rio Grande Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With April temperatures remaining well above normal and limited rainfall the last week of April, the southeastern half of Cameron County remained in Extreme (D3) Drought conditions, while the rest of the county remained in Severe (D2) Drought conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed during the late evening across the northeast Texas Panhandle as a cold front encountered an unstable airmass. A few reports of hail were received, but one storm in particular produced wind-driven large hail and destructive winds. Following the frontal passage, rapidly rising pressures within a tightening pressure gradient led to widespread strong winds across the western 4/5ths of the Texas Panhandle. Between 9:00 PM CST on May 7 and 2:00 AM CST on May 8, sustained winds of 40 to 52 MPH and gusts of 58 to 66 MPH were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small old wooden barn was destroyed. Sections of sheet metal were stripped off a larger barn. Several large tree limbs were broken. Several free-standing old fence posts were snapped. A large horse trailer (28 feet long) which included living quarters was flipped and pushed 8 feet. This event occurred 20 miles southwest of Wolf Creek Park.
