EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed in the afternoon and produced large hail, funnel clouds, wind damage and a long-track tornado. The tornado killed 3 people near Onalaska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado touched down on the eastern shore of Lake Livingston, just NW of Onalaska then moved SE across the town then ESE across Polk County. Some of the most significant damage was in Paradise Acres area of Onalaska. Winds possibly reached 140 mph as the storm moved across this location, which was the only EF3 damage the NWS could confirm along the 30 mile track. The tornado killed three and injured 33 in Onalaska with 291 homes affected and 46 destroyed. It was estimated to be about 500 yards wide as it moved across the town. The tornado continued a general east-|northeastward track across rural Polk county where it snapped and uprooted numerous hardwood and softwood trees and intermittently damaged structures. The tornado path was measured at just over a half-mile wide as it crossed Highway 59 near Seven Oaks, where EF2 damage was surveyed with destruction of two mobile homes and |damage to hardwood trees. The tornado remained in rural areas until it ended very close to Barnes in northeast Polk county. Near the end of the track, a large tree destroyed a mobile home and miraculously just missed a man who was sitting at his computer. He was not seriously injured. For historical context, this was the deadliest single tornado for the NWS Houston-Galveston forecast area since Nov 15th, 1987 when a tornado also killed three in Madison County. The deadliest tornado on record for this area is the F4 in Galveston which killed 8 during Hurricane Carla in 1961.  This is only the 8th multiple-fatality tornado for counties served by our office since 1950.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small tornado touched down near Round Mountain in northern Blanco County. It first appeared to touch down near RR 962 then moved northeast by Coyote Trail before impacting two RV parks on Hwy 281 and Hof Brau. There were two minor injuries. The tornado continued moving northeast and crossed the Blanco/Burnet County line near Creek Ln. and then dissipated in southern Burnet County near CR 401 S.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level shortwave trough moved around a low over the southwestern US and enhanced lift over the mountains in northern Mexico and a dryline in West Texas. These features interacted with a warm, moist airmass over South Central Texas to generate thunderstorms. Some of these storms produced tornadoes and large hail. The large hail that hit the City of Del Rio and near Brackettville likely caused in excess of 10 million dollars in damage but as of this writing do not have an accurate estimate of damage from city/county/state officials or private insurance companies.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COOP observer on the north side of Del Rio reported a thunderstorm producing golf ball size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed as an upper level shortwave trough moved across South Central Texas. These storms fed off a very moist airmass with precipitable water values above two inches. Some storms produced heavy rain that led to flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain that led to flash flooding. Two low water crossings were flooded along Schubert Ln. in Niederwald. These reports were from ATXFloods.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed as an upper level shortwave trough moved across South Central Texas. These storms fed off a very moist airmass with precipitable water values above two inches. Some storms produced heavy rain that led to flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Nearly all of South Central Texas had less than normal rainfall during June. However, the deficit wasn't enough to change the drought status across the region. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With April temperatures remaining well above normal and limited rainfall the last week of April, Severe (D2) to Extreme (D3) Drought persisted into the second week of May. Extreme (D3) drought conditions continued across southern Zapata, western Starr, southeastern Jim Hogg, along the Rio Grande in southern Starr County, and across southeastern Cameron County. Severe (D2) drought conditions continued across central Zapata, southwestern Jim Hogg, northwestern Brooks, northern Kenedy, south and southwestern Willacy, the remainder of Cameron, and majority of the remainder of both Starr and Hidalgo counties. These drought conditions affected agricultural interests and grasslands as well as hydrologic and ecologic interests across most of Deep South Texas and the Rio Grande Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With April temperatures remaining well above normal and limited rainfall the last week of April, the southwestern third of Jim Hogg County remained in Severe (D2) Drought, with a small portion in Extreme (D3) Drought through May 11th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first round of severe weather occurred during the early morning hours of April 28th as a weak upper level disturbance allowed isolated storms to form near a dryline. One storm in particular moved into North Texas and produced severe winds and large hail. Later on the 28th, a deep upper level trough in the Southern Plains allowed convection to initiate along a southward moving cold front. This line of severe storms produced damaging winds and severe hail in North and Central Texas overnight and into the morning of April 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter measured 66 mph winds with a handheld anemometer.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lot of half inch size hail stones and a few stones up to the size of a quarter fell in Texline.

Later on the 28th, a deep upper level trough in the Southern Plains allowed convection to initiate along a southward moving cold front. This line of severe storms produced damaging winds and severe hail in North and Central Texas overnight and into the morning of April 29th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along and east of the dryline across the Texas Panhandle within a moist and strongly unstable airmass. Some of the thunderstorms across the northeast and east-central Texas Panhandle became severe, producing large hail and severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A telephone pole was blown down along US Highway 287 southeast of Lelia Lake near County Road 20.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level shortwave trough moved around a low over the southwestern US and enhanced lift over the mountains in northern Mexico and a dryline in West Texas. These features interacted with a warm, moist airmass over South Central Texas to generate thunderstorms. Some of these storms produced tornadoes and large hail. The large hail that hit the City of Del Rio and near Brackettville likely caused in excess of 10 million dollars in damage but as of this writing do not have an accurate estimate of damage from city/county/state officials or private insurance companies.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced baseball size hail in Del Rio. There was a picture of the hail on Twitter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms which developed each afternoon along a stationary front in the Plains organized into complexes of storms while diving south of the Red River each night. Instances of wind damage and flooding occurred in a few cases.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 63 MPH wind gust was reported by the Automated Surface Observation System at Dallas-Fort Worth International Airport. Trees were reported down just outside the airport property on Bedford Road in Bedford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and moderately unstable airmass which was in place east of the dryline became more unstable during the afternoon as surface temperatures rose into the 97 to 105 degree range.  High-based thunderstorms developed within a moderately-sheared atmosphere as a subtle ridge-riding mid-level disturbance moved across the Texas and Oklahoma Panhandles.  Isolated severe hail occurred during the early- to mid-afternoon over the south-central Texas Panhandle.  Scattered incidents of severe hail during the mid-afternoon to early evening occurred over the northeast and east-central Texas Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 11 miles southwest of Wolf Creek Park, mostly pea to dime size hail with a few stones as large as quarters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The southern half of South Central Texas had well below normal precipitation during February. Much of this region saw less than 25% of normal. The lack of precipitation had the drought deteriorate to Extreme (D3) drought in Atascosa, Dimmit, Frio, Gonzales, Karnes, and Lavaca Counties. Maverick and Zavala Counties remained in D3 and Caldwell, De Witt, Guadalupe, Kinney, Uvalde, Val Verde, and Wilson stayed in Severe (D2) drought.  Larger public water systems in Karnes and Uvalde Counties were in Stage 1 water restrictions and in Val Verde and Zavala there were voluntary restrictions. Lake Amistad was 31.2 feet below Conservation Pool elevation. Of the counties in D2 or worse drought Atascosa, Dimmit, Frio, Karnes, Kinney, Maverick, Uvalde, Val Verde, and Zavala had burn bans in effect. The Texas Crop and Weather Report from Texas A&M Agrilife stated that livestock were in fair condition and producers continued with supplemental feeding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The southern half of South Central Texas had well below normal precipitation during February. Much of this region saw less than 25% of normal. The lack of precipitation had the drought deteriorate to Extreme (D3) drought in Atascosa, Dimmit, Frio, Gonzales, Karnes, and Lavaca Counties. Larger public water systems in Karnes and Uvalde Counties were in Stage 1 water restrictions and in Val Verde and Zavala there were voluntary restrictions. Lake Amistad was 31.2 feet below Conservation Pool elevation. Of the counties in D2 or worse drought Atascosa, Dimmit, Frio, Karnes, Kinney, Maverick, Uvalde, Val Verde, and Zavala had burn bans in effect. The Texas Crop and Weather Report from Texas A&M Agrilife stated that livestock were in fair condition and producers continued with supplemental feeding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of showers remained persistent across much of the Ark-La-Tex through much of the day on February 11th, along the subtropical jet stream that extended over much of Texas and the Lower Mississippi Valley ahead of a strong upper level low pressure system just off the Northwest Baja California coast. These rains primed the already wet soils on the 11th, before the low pressure system began to eject northeast and become absorbed into a broad upper trough as it translated east across the Southern Rockies into the Southern Plains during much of the day on the 12th. As this trough approached, an axis of moderate to locally heavy rainfall developed over Northeast Texas and Southern Arkansas, with widespread rainfall amounts of 4-6 inches, with isolated higher amounts up to 8 inches, observed. This resulted in numerous instances of flash flooding across East and Northeast Texas, with numerous roads flooded and closed, and smaller creeks, streams, and bayous spilling above its banks. The flash flooding ended by afternoon as the rains ended, but continued runoff resulted in continued flooding near and along these waterways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Interstate 30 was flooded and closed at the Franklin/Hopkins County line between County Road 1017 and County Road 1016.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several upper level short wave disturbances moved over the region from the 11th into the early morning hours of the 12th. The first of these was early in the morning on the 11th which brought approximately one to three inches of snow to the southern Texas Panhandle and northwestern South Plains. A lull in activity occurred during the afternoon before another upper level disturbance brought additional snow accumulations in the evening of the 11th through the early morning hours of the 12th.||Snowfall totals from NWS cooperative weather observers are listed below:||5.0 inches at Muleshoe (Bailey County), 4.5 inches at Morton (Cochran County), 4.5 inches at Friona (Parmer County), 4.0 inches at Hart (Castro County), 3.8 inches at Dimmitt (Castro County), 3.0 inches at Olton (Lamb County), 3.0 inches at Silverton (Briscoe County), 2.5 inches at Littlefield (Lamb County), 2.4 inches at Plainview (Hale County), 2.0 inches at Turkey (Hall County), 1.5 inches at Northfield (Motley County), 1.5 inches at Ropesville (Hockley County), 1.1 inches at Shallowater (Lubbock County), 1.0 inches at Levelland (Hockley County), and 1.0 inches at Matador (Motley County).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell along and north of line from northern Irion County to Sterling City then northeast to Rotan, Stamford, Haskell and across northern Throckmorton county.  Snowfall amounts in this area ranged from 4 to 10 inches.  East of this line, sleet amounts were heavy, while snowfall amounts were less.  ||This winter storm was caused by an upper level trough moving across the region as cold arctic air invaded West Central Texas.  All universities, schools, county and city offices were closed on February 5 and even into February 6 for those areas that had the heavy snow.  Roads became treacherous especially where the heavy snow fell.  This snow stayed on the ground for a few days afterwards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Weather Service Cooperative Observer in Putnam measured 0.4 inches of sleet and freezing rain, and another inch of snow.  Total depth of ice and snow was 1.4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper-level low pressure center moved east from southern Colorado across south central Kansas through the late afternoon on Tuesday, June 9, 2020. As it did, a surface low deepened and moved east across Kansas pushing a cold front south across the Oklahoma and Texas Panhandles before moving into western Texas. The cold front moved into the Oklahoma Panhandle early in the morning and quickly swept south through through the Texas Panhandle, continuing to move across the region. A strong pressure gradient between the low over Kansas and high pressure surging southeast out of Colorado created very strong winds across the Texas and Oklahoma Panhandles behind the front, which lasted into the early evening.||Across the northern half of the Texas Panhandle, sustained winds increased into the 40 to greater than 50 MPH range out of the north to northwest, with gusts mainly in the 55 to 65 MPH range. Farther south, sustained winds generally ranged from 37 to 46 MPH with gusts mainly in the 50 to 60 MPH range. These strong winds lasted most of the day before finally diminishing from west to east beginning in the late afternoon. Although the relative humidity was only 15 to 35 percent across the northwestern two-thirds of the Texas Panhandle, the high winds and dry fuels associated with recent onset of moderate to extreme drought in the area led to a high wildfire danger across part of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Texas Mesonet station 1mile northwest of Palo Duro Reservoir measured gusts in the 58 to 64 MPH range during this time period. The highest gust of 64 MPH occurred at 1517CST.

The lack of precipitation had the drought deteriorate to Extreme (D3) drought in Atascosa, Dimmit, Frio, Gonzales, Karnes, and Lavaca Counties. Maverick and Zavala Counties remained in D3 and Caldwell, De Witt, Guadalupe, Kinney, Uvalde, Val Verde, and Wilson stayed in Severe (D2) drought.  Larger public water systems in Karnes and Uvalde Counties were in Stage 1 water restrictions and in Val Verde and Zavala there were voluntary restrictions. Lake Amistad was 31.2 feet below Conservation Pool elevation. Of the counties in D2 or worse drought Atascosa, Dimmit, Frio, Karnes, Kinney, Maverick, Uvalde, Val Verde, and Zavala had burn bans in effect. The Texas Crop and Weather Report from Texas A&M Agrilife stated that livestock were in fair condition and producers continued with supplemental feeding.

A strong cold front brought freezing temperatures and scattered precipitation. Precipitation was a mix of rain, sleet, and snow. Sleet was reported in Atascosa, Uvalde, Kendall, Llano, and Lavaca Counties with no accumulation. Sleet and snow were reported in Real, Kerr, Bexar, Guadalupe, Hays, Travis, and Williamson. In Williamson County Weir, Taylor, and Georgetown had 1/4 of snow and Round Rock and Serenada had 0.2. In Travis County Pflugerville saw 1/2 of snow, parts of Austin had 1/4 to 0.4, Jollyville, Sunset Valley, Lost Creek, and West Lake Hills had 0.2, and Jollyville and San Leanna had 0.1. There were reports of only snow in Val Verde, Bandera, Gillespie, Comal, Caldwell, and Bastrop Counties. Camp Swift in Bastrop County had 1/4 of snow. In Hays County Wimberley had 0.1 of snow and in Comal County New Braunfels had 0.1. With the minor accumulations there weren't any impacts from this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought freezing temperatures and scattered precipitation. Precipitation was a mix of rain, sleet, and snow. Sleet was reported in Atascosa County.|With the minor accumulations there weren't any impacts from this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and early evening along and ahead of an outflow-enhanced cold front. Storms developed within a strongly unstable but weakly sheared airmass.  Scattered severe thunderstorms occurred over the south-central and central Texas Panhandle, with isolated coverage across the northwest and southeast Panhandle. One of the strongest storms produced a hail swath with up to 2 inch stones across the city of Amarillo generally along I-40. This was one three separate storm that produced severe hail in Amarillo on this day. In addition, several small and weak landspout type tornadoes were reported with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Curb-to-curb street flooding was observed at Paramount Boulevard and Interstate-40 in Amarillo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Up-slope east to northeast flow associated with a weak frontal boundary across the area assisted in maintaining a moist and unstable atmosphere across the Texas Panhandle. Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon and evening as an upper level disturbance in northwest flow aloft. The strongly sheared environment led to rotating storms, which supported the production of very large hail and damaging winds across western, northern and central parts of the Panhandle. A brief landspout tornado developed in the southwest Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This gust was measured at the West Texas Mesonet station 1 mile west-southwest of Boys Ranch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Well below normal precipitation fell across south Texas during the month of February with temperatures slightly above normal. Severe drought expanded over the Brush Country and portions of the Victoria Crossroads during the month. There were even some small areas of extreme drought over portions of the Brush Country by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought expanded across the county during the month while extreme drought developed over the western part of the county by the end of the month.

A strong upper level trough brought a few rounds of winter weather to North and Central Texas on February 5th and 6th. Early in the event, a mix of freezing rain, sleet and snow was reported, but eventually transitioned to all snow. Snowfall totals were less than 4 inches but Young, Jack and Eastland counties also accumulated 0.5-1 inch of sleet in addition to the snow. The first 2 rounds of winter precipitation brought accumulations to locations along and northwest of a line from Eastland to Fort Worth To Sherman. The last round of snow on the morning of the 6th left accumulations from Bell County to Navarro County and up to Hopkins County. A total of almost 1.5 of snow was reported in Falls and Limestone counties. One fatality occurred in McLennan County due to slick roadways. A 25-year old male on a motorcycle died when he lost control on a slick spot and was thrown from his bike.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 2-4 inches of snow fell in Young County on top of approximately 0.5-1 inch of sleet. The highest totals were across the northern half of the county. Slushy and slick roads were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and moderately unstable airmass which was in place east of the dryline became more unstable during the afternoon as surface temperatures rose into the 97 to 105 degree range.  High-based thunderstorms developed within a moderately-sheared atmosphere as a subtle ridge-riding mid-level disturbance moved across the Texas and Oklahoma Panhandles.  Isolated severe hail occurred during the early- to mid-afternoon over the south-central Texas Panhandle.  Scattered incidents of severe hail during the mid-afternoon to early evening occurred over the northeast and east-central Texas Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mostly dime to nickel size hail with a few quarter size stones fell 7 miles west-northwest of Washburn. The hail fall lasted about 5 minutes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to penny size hail was observed at the National  Weather Service Office in Amarillo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper-level low pressure center moved east from southern Colorado across south central Kansas through the late afternoon on Tuesday, June 9, 2020. As it did, a surface low deepened and moved east across Kansas pushing a cold front south across the Oklahoma and Texas Panhandles before moving into western Texas. The cold front moved into the Oklahoma Panhandle early in the morning and quickly swept south through through the Texas Panhandle, continuing to move across the region. A strong pressure gradient between the low over Kansas and high pressure surging southeast out of Colorado created very strong winds across the Texas and Oklahoma Panhandles behind the front, which lasted into the early evening.||Across the northern half of the Texas Panhandle, sustained winds increased into the 40 to greater than 50 MPH range out of the north to northwest, with gusts mainly in the 55 to 65 MPH range. Farther south, sustained winds generally ranged from 37 to 46 MPH with gusts mainly in the 50 to 60 MPH range. These strong winds lasted most of the day before finally diminishing from west to east beginning in the late afternoon. Although the relative humidity was only 15 to 35 percent across the northwestern two-thirds of the Texas Panhandle, the high winds and dry fuels associated with recent onset of moderate to extreme drought in the area led to a high wildfire danger across part of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 40 to 48 MPH were measured at Moore County Airport, located 3 miles west of Dumas, during this time period. The highest sustained wind of 48 MPH occurred at 1355CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The southern half of South Central Texas had well below normal precipitation during February. Much of this region saw less than 25% of normal. The lack of precipitation had the drought deteriorate to Extreme (D3) drought in Atascosa, Dimmit, Frio, Gonzales, Karnes, and Lavaca Counties. Maverick and Zavala Counties remained in D3 and Caldwell, De Witt, Guadalupe, Kinney, Uvalde, Val Verde, and Wilson stayed in Severe (D2) drought.  Larger public water systems in Karnes and Uvalde Counties were in Stage 1 water restrictions and in Val Verde and Zavala there were voluntary restrictions. Lake Amistad was 31.2 feet below Conservation Pool elevation. Of the counties in D2 or worse drought Atascosa, Dimmit, Frio, Karnes, Kinney, Maverick, Uvalde, Val Verde, and Zavala had burn bans in effect. The Texas Crop and Weather Report from Texas A&M Agrilife stated that livestock were in fair condition and producers continued with supplemental feeding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of showers remained persistent across much of the Ark-La-Tex through much of the day on February 11th, along the subtropical jet stream that extended over much of Texas and the Lower Mississippi Valley ahead of a strong upper level low pressure system just off the Northwest Baja California coast. These rains primed the already wet soils on the 11th, before the low pressure system began to eject northeast and become absorbed into a broad upper trough as it translated east across the Southern Rockies into the Southern Plains during much of the day on the 12th. As this trough approached, an axis of moderate to locally heavy rainfall developed over Northeast Texas and Southern Arkansas, with widespread rainfall amounts of 4-6 inches, with isolated higher amounts up to 8 inches, observed. This resulted in numerous instances of flash flooding across East and Northeast Texas, with numerous roads flooded and closed, and smaller creeks, streams, and bayous spilling above its banks. The flash flooding ended by afternoon as the rains ended, but continued runoff resulted in continued flooding near and along these waterways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A vehicle was submerged in high water on Greenbriar Road at Butler Creek just north of Lake Placid Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several upper level short wave disturbances moved over the region from the 11th into the early morning hours of the 12th. The first of these was early in the morning on the 11th which brought approximately one to three inches of snow to the southern Texas Panhandle and northwestern South Plains. A lull in activity occurred during the afternoon before another upper level disturbance brought additional snow accumulations in the evening of the 11th through the early morning hours of the 12th.||Snowfall totals from NWS cooperative weather observers are listed below:||5.0 inches at Muleshoe (Bailey County), 4.5 inches at Morton (Cochran County), 4.5 inches at Friona (Parmer County), 4.0 inches at Hart (Castro County), 3.8 inches at Dimmitt (Castro County), 3.0 inches at Olton (Lamb County), 3.0 inches at Silverton (Briscoe County), 2.5 inches at Littlefield (Lamb County), 2.4 inches at Plainview (Hale County), 2.0 inches at Turkey (Hall County), 1.5 inches at Northfield (Motley County), 1.5 inches at Ropesville (Hockley County), 1.1 inches at Shallowater (Lubbock County), 1.0 inches at Levelland (Hockley County), and 1.0 inches at Matador (Motley County).

A strong upper level trough brought a few rounds of winter weather to North and Central Texas on February 5th and 6th. Early in the event, a mix of freezing rain, sleet and snow was reported, but eventually transitioned to all snow. Snowfall totals were less than 4 inches but Young, Jack and Eastland counties also accumulated 0.5-1 inch of sleet in addition to the snow. The first 2 rounds of winter precipitation brought accumulations to locations along and northwest of a line from Eastland to Fort Worth To Sherman. The last round of snow on the morning of the 6th left accumulations from Bell County to Navarro County and up to Hopkins County. A total of almost 1.5 of snow was reported in Falls and Limestone counties. One fatality occurred in McLennan County due to slick roadways. A 25-year old male on a motorcycle died when he lost control on a slick spot and was thrown from his bike.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mix of sleet and snow fell across Jack County. A half inch of sleet was reported in the Bryson area and snowfall totals up to 2-3 inches were reported. The highest snowfall totals were in the northern third of the county. Hazardous travel conditions were also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought freezing temperatures and scattered precipitation. Precipitation was a mix of rain, sleet, and snow. Sleet was reported in Atascosa, Uvalde, Kendall, Llano, and Lavaca Counties with no accumulation. Sleet and snow were reported in Real, Kerr, Bexar, Guadalupe, Hays, Travis, and Williamson. In Williamson County Weir, Taylor, and Georgetown had 1/4 of snow and Round Rock and Serenada had 0.2. In Travis County Pflugerville saw 1/2 of snow, parts of Austin had 1/4 to 0.4, Jollyville, Sunset Valley, Lost Creek, and West Lake Hills had 0.2, and Jollyville and San Leanna had 0.1. There were reports of only snow in Val Verde, Bandera, Gillespie, Comal, Caldwell, and Bastrop Counties. Camp Swift in Bastrop County had 1/4 of snow. In Hays County Wimberley had 0.1 of snow and in Comal County New Braunfels had 0.1. With the minor accumulations there weren't any impacts from this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought freezing temperatures and scattered precipitation. Light snow was reported Comal County.  New Braunfels had 0.1 of snow accumulation. With the minor accumulations there weren't any impacts from this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper-level low pressure center moved east from southern Colorado across south central Kansas through the late afternoon on Tuesday, June 9, 2020. As it did, a surface low deepened and moved east across Kansas pushing a cold front south across the Oklahoma and Texas Panhandles before moving into western Texas. The cold front moved into the Oklahoma Panhandle early in the morning and quickly swept south through through the Texas Panhandle, continuing to move across the region. A strong pressure gradient between the low over Kansas and high pressure surging southeast out of Colorado created very strong winds across the Texas and Oklahoma Panhandles behind the front, which lasted into the early evening.||Across the northern half of the Texas Panhandle, sustained winds increased into the 40 to greater than 50 MPH range out of the north to northwest, with gusts mainly in the 55 to 65 MPH range. Farther south, sustained winds generally ranged from 37 to 46 MPH with gusts mainly in the 50 to 60 MPH range. These strong winds lasted most of the day before finally diminishing from west to east beginning in the late afternoon. Although the relative humidity was only 15 to 35 percent across the northwestern two-thirds of the Texas Panhandle, the high winds and dry fuels associated with recent onset of moderate to extreme drought in the area led to a high wildfire danger across part of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusts of 58 to 62 MPH were measured at Dalhart Municipal Airport, located 3 miles southwest of Dalhart, during this time period. The highest gust of 62 MPH occurred at 1336CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and early evening along and ahead of an outflow-enhanced cold front. Storms developed within a strongly unstable but weakly sheared airmass.  Scattered severe thunderstorms occurred over the south-central and central Texas Panhandle, with isolated coverage across the northwest and southeast Panhandle. One of the strongest storms produced a hail swath with up to 2 inch stones across the city of Amarillo generally along I-40. This was one three separate storm that produced severe hail in Amarillo on this day. In addition, several small and weak landspout type tornadoes were reported with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar size hail fell 6 miles north-northeast of Amarillo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper low developed over the western U.S. and moved into the southern Rockies. At the same time a strong arctic cold front moved through West Texas and southeastern New Mexico bringing temperatures well below freezing. Precipitation began on the evening of the 4th that quickly transitioned to snow overnight that continued well into the afternoon of the 5th causing isolated snowfall amounts of almost a foot, with widespread amounts of 3 to 8 inches reported.

A strong cold front brought freezing temperatures and scattered precipitation. Precipitation was a mix of rain, sleet, and snow. In Travis County Pflugerville saw 1/2 of snow, parts of Austin had 1/4 to 0.4, Jollyville, Sunset Valley, Lost Creek, and West Lake Hills had 0.2, and Jollyville and San Leanna had 0.1. There were reports of only snow in Val Verde, Bandera, Gillespie, Comal, Caldwell, and Bastrop Counties. Camp Swift in Bastrop County had 1/4 of snow. In Hays County Wimberley had 0.1 of snow and in Comal County New Braunfels had 0.1. With the minor accumulations there weren't any impacts from this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought freezing temperatures and scattered precipitation. Precipitation was a mix of rain, sleet, and snow. Sleet was reported in Kendall County.  With the minor accumulations there weren't any impacts from this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper-level low pressure center moved east from southern Colorado across south central Kansas through the late afternoon on Tuesday, June 9, 2020. As it did, a surface low deepened and moved east across Kansas pushing a cold front south across the Oklahoma and Texas Panhandles before moving into western Texas. The cold front moved into the Oklahoma Panhandle early in the morning and quickly swept south through through the Texas Panhandle, continuing to move across the region. A strong pressure gradient between the low over Kansas and high pressure surging southeast out of Colorado created very strong winds across the Texas and Oklahoma Panhandles behind the front, which lasted into the early evening.||Across the northern half of the Texas Panhandle, sustained winds increased into the 40 to greater than 50 MPH range out of the north to northwest, with gusts mainly in the 55 to 65 MPH range. Farther south, sustained winds generally ranged from 37 to 46 MPH with gusts mainly in the 50 to 60 MPH range. These strong winds lasted most of the day before finally diminishing from west to east beginning in the late afternoon. Although the relative humidity was only 15 to 35 percent across the northwestern two-thirds of the Texas Panhandle, the high winds and dry fuels associated with recent onset of moderate to extreme drought in the area led to a high wildfire danger across part of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Texas Mesonet station 1 mile northwest of Palo Duro Reservoir measured sustained winds in the 40 to 54 MPH range during this time period. The highest sustained wind of 54 MPH occurred at 1252CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The southern half of South Central Texas had well below normal precipitation during February. Much of this region saw less than 25% of normal. The lack of precipitation had the drought deteriorate to Extreme (D3) drought in Atascosa, Dimmit, Frio, Gonzales, Karnes, and Lavaca Counties. Maverick and Zavala Counties remained in D3 and Caldwell, De Witt, Guadalupe, Kinney, Uvalde, Val Verde, and Wilson stayed in Severe (D2) drought.  Larger public water systems in Karnes and Uvalde Counties were in Stage 1 water restrictions and in Val Verde and Zavala there were voluntary restrictions. Lake Amistad was 31.2 feet below Conservation Pool elevation. Of the counties in D2 or worse drought Atascosa, Dimmit, Frio, Karnes, Kinney, Maverick, Uvalde, Val Verde, and Zavala had burn bans in effect. The Texas Crop and Weather Report from Texas A&M Agrilife stated that livestock were in fair condition and producers continued with supplemental feeding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The southern half of South Central Texas had well below normal precipitation during February. Much of this region saw less than 25% of normal. Larger public water systems in Karnes and Uvalde Counties were in Stage 1 water restrictions and in Val Verde and Zavala there were voluntary restrictions. Lake Amistad was 31.2 feet below Conservation Pool elevation. Of the counties in D2 or worse drought Atascosa, Dimmit, Frio, Karnes, Kinney, Maverick, Uvalde, Val Verde, and Zavala had burn bans in effect. The Texas Crop and Weather Report from Texas A&M Agrilife stated that livestock were in fair condition and producers continued with supplemental feeding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving trough over the Rockies led to widespread snow, sleet, and freezing rain across the southern Plains. A band of heavy snow fell across portions of western north Texas where 4 to 8 inches of snow was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought stress on ranchlands in Starr, Jim Hogg and Zapata County was maintained with minimal rainfall and mild to warm temperatures; Severe (D2) Drought areal coverage did not change significantly as rainfall favored the coastal counties of the Rio Grande Valley and King Ranch. Extreme Drought (D3) persisted across the western portion of Zapata County. These conditions were maintained through February 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A sliver of Extreme Drought (D3) persisted across western Zapata County, with the remainder of the County in Severe Drought (D2), through the 10th of February, as rain-free and low humidity conditions continued.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe drought (D2) conditions continued to start the month of February across much of Smith and Cherokee Counties in East Texas, despite near to slightly above normal rainfall that fell during January. However, a prolonged period of rain fell across all of East Texas throughout the first three weeks of February, with widespread rainfall amounts of six to in excess of nine inches falling across Smith and Cherokee Counties, as well as the remainder of East Texas. These amounts eliminated the remaining short and longer term dryness that had developed since the late summer of 2019, with considerable runoff resulting in extensive areas of flash flooding across these areas on the 12th, where numerous roads were flooded and closed. The runoff also recharged the area lakes, rivers, creeks, and streams, with much above normal streamflows returning by the third week of the month across the Sabine and Neches River basins. By the end of the month, even abnormally dry (D0) conditions were eliminated across the western sections of East Texas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low over the Four Corners Region quickly moved east into the Central Plains bringing strong winds to the higher elevations of the Guadalupe and Davis Mountains.
An upper low over the Four Corners Region quickly moved east into the Central Plains bringing strong winds to the higher elevations of the Guadalupe and Davis Mountains.

A strong cold front brought freezing temperatures and scattered precipitation. Precipitation was a mix of rain, sleet, and snow. Sleet was reported in Atascosa, Uvalde, Kendall, Llano, and Lavaca Counties with no accumulation. Sleet and snow were reported in Real, Kerr, Bexar, Guadalupe, Hays, Travis, and Williamson. In Williamson County Weir, Taylor, and Georgetown had 1/4 of snow and Round Rock and Serenada had 0.2. In Travis County Pflugerville saw 1/2 of snow, parts of Austin had 1/4 to 0.4, Jollyville, Sunset Valley, Lost Creek, and West Lake Hills had 0.2, and Jollyville and San Leanna had 0.1. There were reports of only snow in Val Verde, Bandera, Gillespie, Comal, Caldwell, and Bastrop Counties. Camp Swift in Bastrop County had 1/4 of snow. In Hays County Wimberley had 0.1 of snow and in Comal County New Braunfels had 0.1. With the minor accumulations there weren't any impacts from this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought freezing temperatures and scattered precipitation. Precipitation was a mix of rain, sleet, and snow.  Sleet and snow were reported in Real County.  With the minor accumulations there weren't any impacts from this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper-level low pressure center moved east from southern Colorado across south central Kansas through the late afternoon on Tuesday, June 9, 2020. As it did, a surface low deepened and moved east across Kansas pushing a cold front south across the Oklahoma and Texas Panhandles before moving into western Texas. The cold front moved into the Oklahoma Panhandle early in the morning and quickly swept south through through the Texas Panhandle, continuing to move across the region. A strong pressure gradient between the low over Kansas and high pressure surging southeast out of Colorado created very strong winds across the Texas and Oklahoma Panhandles behind the front, which lasted into the early evening.||Across the northern half of the Texas Panhandle, sustained winds increased into the 40 to greater than 50 MPH range out of the north to northwest, with gusts mainly in the 55 to 65 MPH range. Farther south, sustained winds generally ranged from 37 to 46 MPH with gusts mainly in the 50 to 60 MPH range. These strong winds lasted most of the day before finally diminishing from west to east beginning in the late afternoon. Although the relative humidity was only 15 to 35 percent across the northwestern two-thirds of the Texas Panhandle, the high winds and dry fuels associated with recent onset of moderate to extreme drought in the area led to a high wildfire danger across part of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Texas Mesonet station 11 miles south-southwest of Romero measured sustained winds in the 40 to 45 MPH range during this time period. The highest sustained wind of 45 MPH occurred at 1216CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and early evening along and ahead of an outflow-enhanced cold front. Storms developed within a strongly unstable but weakly sheared airmass.  Scattered severe thunderstorms occurred over the south-central and central Texas Panhandle, with isolated coverage across the northwest and southeast Panhandle. One of the strongest storms produced a hail swath with up to 2 inch stones across the city of Amarillo generally along I-40. This was one three separate storm that produced severe hail in Amarillo on this day. In addition, several small and weak landspout type tornadoes were reported with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 3 miles northeast of Washburn, between mile markers 88 and 89 on Interstate-40, several  hydroplane-induced accidents were reported, with the eastbound service road being flooded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusts of 58 to 62 MPH were measured at Dalhart Municipal Airport for several hours. While this site is 2 miles south of the county line, observation sites in all adjacent counties also either reported winds gusts in excess of 58 mph and/or sustained winds of 40 mph or greater. It was reported that many cotton fields with young plants were a complete loss as the plants were sheared apart to ground level by the combination of high winds and blowing dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The southern half of South Central Texas had well below normal precipitation during February. Much of this region saw less than 25% of normal. The lack of precipitation had the drought deteriorate to Extreme (D3) drought in Atascosa, Dimmit, Frio, Gonzales, Karnes, and Lavaca Counties. Maverick and Zavala Counties remained in D3 and Caldwell, De Witt, Guadalupe, Kinney, Uvalde, Val Verde, and Wilson stayed in Severe (D2) drought.  Larger public water systems in Karnes and Uvalde Counties were in Stage 1 water restrictions and in Val Verde and Zavala there were voluntary restrictions. Lake Amistad was 31.2 feet below Conservation Pool elevation. Of the counties in D2 or worse drought Atascosa, Dimmit, Frio, Karnes, Kinney, Maverick, Uvalde, Val Verde, and Zavala had burn bans in effect. The Texas Crop and Weather Report from Texas A&M Agrilife stated that livestock were in fair condition and producers continued with supplemental feeding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The southern half of South Central Texas had well below normal precipitation during February. Much of this region saw less than 25% of normal. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening thunderstorm complex produced gusty winds as it moved southeast into North Texas Thursday night, causing isolated damage in Montague County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nocona police reported a metal carport was blown into the street near Nocona High School.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The summer heat really started showing in late June and early July. A Heat Advisory for Heat Index values over 105 degrees was issued beginning June 30th and continuing into the first few days of July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Afternoon Heat Index values soared to near 105 degrees. A Heat Advisory was in effect through July 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of showers remained persistent across much of the Ark-La-Tex through much of the day on February 11th, along the subtropical jet stream that extended over much of Texas and the Lower Mississippi Valley ahead of a strong upper level low pressure system just off the Northwest Baja California coast. These rains primed the already wet soils on the 11th, before the low pressure system began to eject northeast and become absorbed into a broad upper trough as it translated east across the Southern Rockies into the Southern Plains during much of the day on the 12th. As this trough approached, an axis of moderate to locally heavy rainfall developed over Northeast Texas and Southern Arkansas, with widespread rainfall amounts of 4-6 inches, with isolated higher amounts up to 8 inches, observed. This resulted in numerous instances of flash flooding across East and Northeast Texas, with numerous roads flooded and closed, and smaller creeks, streams, and bayous spilling above its banks. The flash flooding ended by afternoon as the rains ended, but continued runoff resulted in continued flooding near and along these waterways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 271 at Highway 80 in Gladewater was flooded and closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system was moving across southern Kansas on the afternoon of the 25th with a short wave moving around the southwestern quadrant of the system. Strong winds aloft were able to mix down to the surface by the afternoon across much of the Texas Panhandle. These strong winds extended into the extreme southern Texas Panhandle where sustained winds of 40 mph and greater were observed for several hours. Several Texas Tech University West Texas mesonet sites in the extreme southern Texas Panhandle measured sustained winds at or above 40 mph for several hours. The peak sustained wind reports are listed below:||47 mph at Happy (Swisher County), 46 mph at Vigo Park (Swisher County), 44 mph at Tulia (Swisher County), 44 mph at Lesley (Hall County), and 42 mph at Aiken (Hale County). Additionally, the Automated Surface Observing System (ASOS) at Childress Municipal Airport recorded a wind gust to 61 mph.

On the fourth, a large upper level storm system was moving southeast across the Intermountain West. Out ahead of this storm system was a strong stream of moisture with embedded areas of lift associated with weaker short waves. The first of these short waves moved over south-central New Mexico into the southwestern Texas Panhandle. Moderate to heavy snow fell out of the concentrated band of precipitation from Friona through Dimmitt and Tulia. Snow totals of three to five inches were common by the time this snow band finally diminished late on the fourth. After a brief lull in activity, the main upper level storm system began to approach from the west. This caused a broad area of precipitation to develop across Far West Texas and the Permian Basin Tuesday night. Initially, some cold rain fell as it spread northeast, but it quickly changed to all snow over the South Plains and Rolling Plains as cold air deepened. This long-duration shield of snow was directed at the Rolling Plains, where snow persisted from Tuesday night through much of the day Wednesday before finally ending by evening. Most of the central and southern Rolling Plains tallied an impressive six to 12 inches of snow, double to triple their average seasonal snow totals.||Snowfall totals are listed below:||14.0 inches at Jayton (Kent County), 10.6 inches at Aspermont (Stonewall County), 9.5 inches at Lake Alan Henry (Garza County), 8.0 inches at Guthrie (King County), 7.5 inches at Post (Garza County), 7.0 inches at Spur (Dickens County), 5.5 inches at Friona (Parmer County), 4.5 inches at Denver City (Yoakum County), 4.5 inches at Tahoka (Lynn County), 4.0 inches at Paducah 10S (Cottle County), 4.0 inches at Hart (Castro County), 3.7 inches at Slaton (Lubbock County), 3.3 inches at Brownfield (Terry County), 3.0 inches at Kirkland (Childress County), 3.0 inches at Tulia (Swisher County), 2.7 inches at Muleshoe (Bailey County), 2.5 inches at Crosbyton (Crosby County), 2.0 inches at Roaring Springs (Motley County), 1.5 inches at Levelland (Hockley County), 1.3 inches at Childress (Childress County), 1.0 inches at Olton (Lamb County), and 1.0 inches at Lubbock (Lubbock County).

An upper low over the Four Corners Region quickly moved east into the Central Plains bringing strong winds to the higher elevations of the Guadalupe and Davis Mountains.

A slow moving trough over the Rockies led to widespread snow, sleet, and freezing rain across the southern Plains. A band of heavy snow fell across portions of western north Texas where 4 to 8 inches of snow was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to six inches of snow fell across the county, with six inches recorded 1 mile northwest of Lakeside City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought freezing temperatures and scattered precipitation. Precipitation was a mix of rain, sleet, and snow. Sleet was reported in Atascosa, Uvalde, Kendall, Llano, and Lavaca Counties with no accumulation. Sleet and snow were reported in Real, Kerr, Bexar, Guadalupe, Hays, Travis, and Williamson. In Williamson County Weir, Taylor, and Georgetown had 1/4 of snow and Round Rock and Serenada had 0.2. In Travis County Pflugerville saw 1/2 of snow, parts of Austin had 1/4 to 0.4, Jollyville, Sunset Valley, Lost Creek, and West Lake Hills had 0.2, and Jollyville and San Leanna had 0.1. There were reports of only snow in Val Verde, Bandera, Gillespie, Comal, Caldwell, and Bastrop Counties. Camp Swift in Bastrop County had 1/4 of snow. In Hays County Wimberley had 0.1 of snow and in Comal County New Braunfels had 0.1. With the minor accumulations there weren't any impacts from this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought freezing temperatures and scattered precipitation. Precipitation was a mix of rain, sleet, and snow.  There were reports of only light snow in Val Verde County.  With the minor accumulations there weren't any impacts from this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The summer heat really started showing in late June and early July. A Heat Advisory for Heat Index values over 105 degrees was issued beginning June 30th and continuing into the first few days of July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Afternoon Heat Index values soared to near 110 degrees. A Heat Advisory was in effect through July 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and early evening along and ahead of an outflow-enhanced cold front. Storms developed within a strongly unstable but weakly sheared airmass.  Scattered severe thunderstorms occurred over the south-central and central Texas Panhandle, with isolated coverage across the northwest and southeast Panhandle. One of the strongest storms produced a hail swath with up to 2 inch stones across the city of Amarillo generally along I-40. This was one three separate storm that produced severe hail in Amarillo on this day. In addition, several small and weak landspout type tornadoes were reported with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At one mile north-northeast of Howardwick, dime to quarter size hail fell, with up to an inch of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and moderately unstable airmass which was in place east of the dryline became more unstable during the afternoon as surface temperatures rose into the 97 to 105 degree range.  High-based thunderstorms developed within a moderately-sheared atmosphere as a subtle ridge-riding mid-level disturbance moved across the Texas and Oklahoma Panhandles.  Isolated severe hail occurred during the early- to mid-afternoon over the south-central Texas Panhandle.  Scattered incidents of severe hail during the mid-afternoon to early evening occurred over the northeast and east-central Texas Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to quarter size hail was reported at the Pantex plant.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The southern half of South Central Texas had well below normal precipitation during February. Much of this region saw less than 25% of normal. The lack of precipitation had the drought deteriorate to Extreme (D3) drought in Atascosa, Dimmit, Frio, Gonzales, Karnes, and Lavaca Counties. Maverick and Zavala Counties remained in D3 and Caldwell, De Witt, Guadalupe, Kinney, Uvalde, Val Verde, and Wilson stayed in Severe (D2) drought.  Larger public water systems in Karnes and Uvalde Counties were in Stage 1 water restrictions and in Val Verde and Zavala there were voluntary restrictions. Lake Amistad was 31.2 feet below Conservation Pool elevation. Of the counties in D2 or worse drought Atascosa, Dimmit, Frio, Karnes, Kinney, Maverick, Uvalde, Val Verde, and Zavala had burn bans in effect. The Texas Crop and Weather Report from Texas A&M Agrilife stated that livestock were in fair condition and producers continued with supplemental feeding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of showers remained persistent across much of the Ark-La-Tex through much of the day on February 11th, along the subtropical jet stream that extended over much of Texas and the Lower Mississippi Valley ahead of a strong upper level low pressure system just off the Northwest Baja California coast. These rains primed the already wet soils on the 11th, before the low pressure system began to eject northeast and become absorbed into a broad upper trough as it translated east across the Southern Rockies into the Southern Plains during much of the day on the 12th. As this trough approached, an axis of moderate to locally heavy rainfall developed over Northeast Texas and Southern Arkansas, with widespread rainfall amounts of 4-6 inches, with isolated higher amounts up to 8 inches, observed. This resulted in numerous instances of flash flooding across East and Northeast Texas, with numerous roads flooded and closed, and smaller creeks, streams, and bayous spilling above its banks. The flash flooding ended by afternoon as the rains ended, but continued runoff resulted in continued flooding near and along these waterways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cumberland Road was closed due to flooding from Shackleford Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system was moving across southern Kansas on the afternoon of the 25th with a short wave moving around the southwestern quadrant of the system. Strong winds aloft were able to mix down to the surface by the afternoon across much of the Texas Panhandle. These strong winds extended into the extreme southern Texas Panhandle where sustained winds of 40 mph and greater were observed for several hours. Several Texas Tech University West Texas mesonet sites in the extreme southern Texas Panhandle measured sustained winds at or above 40 mph for several hours. The peak sustained wind reports are listed below:||47 mph at Happy (Swisher County), 46 mph at Vigo Park (Swisher County), 44 mph at Tulia (Swisher County), 44 mph at Lesley (Hall County), and 42 mph at Aiken (Hale County). Additionally, the Automated Surface Observing System (ASOS) at Childress Municipal Airport recorded a wind gust to 61 mph.

On the fourth, a large upper level storm system was moving southeast across the Intermountain West. Out ahead of this storm system was a strong stream of moisture with embedded areas of lift associated with weaker short waves. The first of these short waves moved over south-central New Mexico into the southwestern Texas Panhandle. Moderate to heavy snow fell out of the concentrated band of precipitation from Friona through Dimmitt and Tulia. Snow totals of three to five inches were common by the time this snow band finally diminished late on the fourth. After a brief lull in activity, the main upper level storm system began to approach from the west. This caused a broad area of precipitation to develop across Far West Texas and the Permian Basin Tuesday night. Initially, some cold rain fell as it spread northeast, but it quickly changed to all snow over the South Plains and Rolling Plains as cold air deepened. This long-duration shield of snow was directed at the Rolling Plains, where snow persisted from Tuesday night through much of the day Wednesday before finally ending by evening. Most of the central and southern Rolling Plains tallied an impressive six to 12 inches of snow, double to triple their average seasonal snow totals.||Snowfall totals are listed below:||14.0 inches at Jayton (Kent County), 10.6 inches at Aspermont (Stonewall County), 9.5 inches at Lake Alan Henry (Garza County), 8.0 inches at Guthrie (King County), 7.5 inches at Post (Garza County), 7.0 inches at Spur (Dickens County), 5.5 inches at Friona (Parmer County), 4.5 inches at Denver City (Yoakum County), 4.5 inches at Tahoka (Lynn County), 4.0 inches at Paducah 10S (Cottle County), 4.0 inches at Hart (Castro County), 3.7 inches at Slaton (Lubbock County), 3.3 inches at Brownfield (Terry County), 3.0 inches at Kirkland (Childress County), 3.0 inches at Tulia (Swisher County), 2.7 inches at Muleshoe (Bailey County), 2.5 inches at Crosbyton (Crosby County), 2.0 inches at Roaring Springs (Motley County), 1.5 inches at Levelland (Hockley County), 1.3 inches at Childress (Childress County), 1.0 inches at Olton (Lamb County), and 1.0 inches at Lubbock (Lubbock County).

A strong cold front brought freezing temperatures and scattered precipitation. Precipitation was a mix of rain, sleet, and snow. Sleet was reported in Atascosa, Uvalde, Kendall, Llano, and Lavaca Counties with no accumulation. Sleet and snow were reported in Real, Kerr, Bexar, Guadalupe, Hays, Travis, and Williamson. In Williamson County Weir, Taylor, and Georgetown had 1/4 of snow and Round Rock and Serenada had 0.2. In Travis County Pflugerville saw 1/2 of snow, parts of Austin had 1/4 to 0.4, Jollyville, Sunset Valley, Lost Creek, and West Lake Hills had 0.2, and Jollyville and San Leanna had 0.1. There were reports of only snow in Val Verde, Bandera, Gillespie, Comal, Caldwell, and Bastrop Counties. Camp Swift in Bastrop County had 1/4 of snow. In Hays County Wimberley had 0.1 of snow and in Comal County New Braunfels had 0.1. With the minor accumulations there weren't any impacts from this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought freezing temperatures and scattered precipitation. Precipitation was a mix of rain, sleet, and snow.  Sleet and snow were reported in  Hays, Travis, and Williamson Counties. In Williamson County Weir, Taylor, and Georgetown had 1/4 of snow and Round Rock and Serenada had 0.2. In Travis County Pflugerville saw 1/2 of snow, parts of Austin had 1/4 to 0.4, Jollyville, Sunset Valley, Lost Creek, and West Lake Hills had 0.2, and Jollyville and San Leanna had 0.1. Camp Swift in Bastrop County had 1/4 of snow. In Hays County Wimberley had 0.1 of snow and in Comal County New Braunfels had 0.1. With the minor accumulations there weren't any impacts from this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell along and north of line from northern Irion County to Sterling City then northeast to Rotan, Stamford, Haskell and across northern Throckmorton county.  Snowfall amounts in this area ranged from 4 to 10 inches.  East of this line, sleet amounts were heavy, while snowfall amounts were less.  ||This winter storm was caused by an upper level trough moving across the region as cold arctic air invaded West Central Texas.  All universities, schools, county and city offices were closed on February 5 and even into February 6 for those areas that had the heavy snow.  Roads became treacherous especially where the heavy snow fell.  This snow stayed on the ground for a few days afterwards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Weather Service Cooperative Observer in Rotan measured 10 inches of snow, while Roby reported 5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and early evening along and ahead of an outflow-enhanced cold front. Storms developed within a strongly unstable but weakly sheared airmass.  Scattered severe thunderstorms occurred over the south-central and central Texas Panhandle, with isolated coverage across the northwest and southeast Panhandle. One of the strongest storms produced a hail swath with up to 2 inch stones across the city of Amarillo generally along I-40. This was one three separate storm that produced severe hail in Amarillo on this day. In addition, several small and weak landspout type tornadoes were reported with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A second round of half dollar size hail fell at the NWS Forecast Office at Amarillo, lasting 7 minutes. This hail fell from a second storm that developed in the wake of the storm that produced the first hail event during the previous hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The southern half of South Central Texas had well below normal precipitation during February. Much of this region saw less than 25% of normal. The lack of precipitation had the drought deteriorate to Extreme (D3) drought in Atascosa, Dimmit, Frio, Gonzales, Karnes, and Lavaca Counties. Maverick and Zavala Counties remained in D3 and Caldwell, De Witt, Guadalupe, Kinney, Uvalde, Val Verde, and Wilson stayed in Severe (D2) drought.  Larger public water systems in Karnes and Uvalde Counties were in Stage 1 water restrictions and in Val Verde and Zavala there were voluntary restrictions. Lake Amistad was 31.2 feet below Conservation Pool elevation. Of the counties in D2 or worse drought Atascosa, Dimmit, Frio, Karnes, Kinney, Maverick, Uvalde, Val Verde, and Zavala had burn bans in effect. The Texas Crop and Weather Report from Texas A&M Agrilife stated that livestock were in fair condition and producers continued with supplemental feeding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of showers remained persistent across much of the Ark-La-Tex through much of the day on February 11th, along the subtropical jet stream that extended over much of Texas and the Lower Mississippi Valley ahead of a strong upper level low pressure system just off the Northwest Baja California coast. These rains primed the already wet soils on the 11th, before the low pressure system began to eject northeast and become absorbed into a broad upper trough as it translated east across the Southern Rockies into the Southern Plains during much of the day on the 12th. As this trough approached, an axis of moderate to locally heavy rainfall developed over Northeast Texas and Southern Arkansas, with widespread rainfall amounts of 4-6 inches, with isolated higher amounts up to 8 inches, observed. This resulted in numerous instances of flash flooding across East and Northeast Texas, with numerous roads flooded and closed, and smaller creeks, streams, and bayous spilling above its banks. The flash flooding ended by afternoon as the rains ended, but continued runoff resulted in continued flooding near and along these waterways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County Road 1640 was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Well below normal precipitation fell across south Texas during the month of February with temperatures slightly above normal. Severe drought expanded over the Brush Country and portions of the Victoria Crossroads during the month. There were even some small areas of extreme drought over portions of the Brush Country by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought expanded across the county during the month while extreme drought developed over the western part of the county by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper-level low pressure center moved east from southern Colorado across south central Kansas through the late afternoon on Tuesday, June 9, 2020. As it did, a surface low deepened and moved east across Kansas pushing a cold front south across the Oklahoma and Texas Panhandles before moving into western Texas. The cold front moved into the Oklahoma Panhandle early in the morning and quickly swept south through through the Texas Panhandle, continuing to move across the region. A strong pressure gradient between the low over Kansas and high pressure surging southeast out of Colorado created very strong winds across the Texas and Oklahoma Panhandles behind the front, which lasted into the early evening.||Across the northern half of the Texas Panhandle, sustained winds increased into the 40 to greater than 50 MPH range out of the north to northwest, with gusts mainly in the 55 to 65 MPH range. Farther south, sustained winds generally ranged from 37 to 46 MPH with gusts mainly in the 50 to 60 MPH range. These strong winds lasted most of the day before finally diminishing from west to east beginning in the late afternoon. Although the relative humidity was only 15 to 35 percent across the northwestern two-thirds of the Texas Panhandle, the high winds and dry fuels associated with recent onset of moderate to extreme drought in the area led to a high wildfire danger across part of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Texas Mesonet station 4 miles west-northwest of Stratford measured sustained winds in the 40 to 54 MPH range during this time period. The highest sustained wind of 54 MPH occurred at 1200CST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three flooded locations were reported in Flower Mound: Bridlewood at W. Windsor, Hwy 377 at 1171, and Lusk Lane near Kings Road. On Lusk Lane, at least one car was pushed to the side of the road and the driver was stranded. The water over the bridge was about 1 foot deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and moderately unstable airmass which was in place east of the dryline became more unstable during the afternoon as surface temperatures rose into the 97 to 105 degree range.  High-based thunderstorms developed within a moderately-sheared atmosphere as a subtle ridge-riding mid-level disturbance moved across the Texas and Oklahoma Panhandles.  Isolated severe hail occurred during the early- to mid-afternoon over the south-central Texas Panhandle.  Scattered incidents of severe hail during the mid-afternoon to early evening occurred over the northeast and east-central Texas Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mostly pea to dime size hail with some hail up to the size of nickels fell 4 miles northwest of Washburn. Hail accumulated to the point of completely covering the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy rainfall across the Neches River basin of East Texas throughout much of February resulted in moderate flooding along the Neches River near Alto.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread rainfall amounts of five to seven inches fell across the Neches River basin of East Texas during February. As a result, the Neches River southwest of Alto near Highway 21 rose above the 16 foot flood stage at 10 am on February 19th, reached the 18 foot moderate flood stage at 4 am on the 21st, before reaching a flat crest of 18.4 feet on the 22nd through the 24th. The Neches River remained near 18 feet through 6 pm on the 26th, before slowly falling through the remainder of the month. ||This flooding resulted in moderate to severe flooding of the heavily wooded floodplain, with boat ramps and picnic areas completely inundated.

Snowfall amounts in this area ranged from 4 to 10 inches.  East of this line, sleet amounts were heavy, while snowfall amounts were less.  ||This winter storm was caused by an upper level trough moving across the region as cold arctic air invaded West Central Texas.  All universities, schools, county and city offices were closed on February 5 and even into February 6 for those areas that had the heavy snow.  Roads became treacherous especially where the heavy snow fell.  This snow stayed on the ground for a few days afterwards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Weather Service Cooperative Observer in Water Valley measured 2 inches of snow.  Christoval reported about an inch of snow, while National Weather Service in San Angelo measured about a tenth of an inch of sleet and snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms which developed each afternoon along a stationary front in the Plains organized into complexes of storms while diving south of the Red River each night. Instances of wind damage and flooding occurred in a few cases.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The weather station at KXII reported 60 mph winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper-level low pressure center moved east from southern Colorado across south central Kansas through the late afternoon on Tuesday, June 9, 2020. As it did, a surface low deepened and moved east across Kansas pushing a cold front south across the Oklahoma and Texas Panhandles before moving into western Texas. The cold front moved into the Oklahoma Panhandle early in the morning and quickly swept south through through the Texas Panhandle, continuing to move across the region. A strong pressure gradient between the low over Kansas and high pressure surging southeast out of Colorado created very strong winds across the Texas and Oklahoma Panhandles behind the front, which lasted into the early evening.||Across the northern half of the Texas Panhandle, sustained winds increased into the 40 to greater than 50 MPH range out of the north to northwest, with gusts mainly in the 55 to 65 MPH range. Farther south, sustained winds generally ranged from 37 to 46 MPH with gusts mainly in the 50 to 60 MPH range. These strong winds lasted most of the day before finally diminishing from west to east beginning in the late afternoon. Although the relative humidity was only 15 to 35 percent across the northwestern two-thirds of the Texas Panhandle, the high winds and dry fuels associated with recent onset of moderate to extreme drought in the area led to a high wildfire danger across part of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Texas Mesonet station 9 miles north-northeast of Amarillo measured sustained winds in the 40 to 43 MPH range during this time period. The highest sustained winds of 43 MPH occurred at 1405CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The southern half of South Central Texas had well below normal precipitation during February. Much of this region saw less than 25% of normal. The lack of precipitation had the drought deteriorate to Extreme (D3) drought in Atascosa, Dimmit, Frio, Gonzales, Karnes, and Lavaca Counties. Maverick and Zavala Counties remained in D3 and Caldwell, De Witt, Guadalupe, Kinney, Uvalde, Val Verde, and Wilson stayed in Severe (D2) drought.  Larger public water systems in Karnes and Uvalde Counties were in Stage 1 water restrictions and in Val Verde and Zavala there were voluntary restrictions. Lake Amistad was 31.2 feet below Conservation Pool elevation. Of the counties in D2 or worse drought Atascosa, Dimmit, Frio, Karnes, Kinney, Maverick, Uvalde, Val Verde, and Zavala had burn bans in effect. The Texas Crop and Weather Report from Texas A&M Agrilife stated that livestock were in fair condition and producers continued with supplemental feeding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The southern half of South Central Texas had well below normal precipitation during February. Much of this region saw less than 25% of normal. Lake Amistad was 31.2 feet below Conservation Pool elevation. Of the counties in D2 or worse drought Atascosa, Dimmit, Frio, Karnes, Kinney, Maverick, Uvalde, Val Verde, and Zavala had burn bans in effect. The Texas Crop and Weather Report from Texas A&M Agrilife stated that livestock were in fair condition and producers continued with supplemental feeding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of showers remained persistent across much of the Ark-La-Tex through much of the day on February 11th, along the subtropical jet stream that extended over much of Texas and the Lower Mississippi Valley ahead of a strong upper level low pressure system just off the Northwest Baja California coast. These rains primed the already wet soils on the 11th, before the low pressure system began to eject northeast and become absorbed into a broad upper trough as it translated east across the Southern Rockies into the Southern Plains during much of the day on the 12th. As this trough approached, an axis of moderate to locally heavy rainfall developed over Northeast Texas and Southern Arkansas, with widespread rainfall amounts of 4-6 inches, with isolated higher amounts up to 8 inches, observed. This resulted in numerous instances of flash flooding across East and Northeast Texas, with numerous roads flooded and closed, and smaller creeks, streams, and bayous spilling above its banks. The flash flooding ended by afternoon as the rains ended, but continued runoff resulted in continued flooding near and along these waterways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads were flooded and closed throughout Upshur County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and early evening along and ahead of an outflow-enhanced cold front. Storms developed within a strongly unstable but weakly sheared airmass.  Scattered severe thunderstorms occurred over the south-central and central Texas Panhandle, with isolated coverage across the northwest and southeast Panhandle. One of the strongest storms produced a hail swath with up to 2 inch stones across the city of Amarillo generally along I-40. This was one three separate storm that produced severe hail in Amarillo on this day. In addition, several small and weak landspout type tornadoes were reported with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail fell 1 mile east of Ware.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several upper level short wave disturbances moved over the region from the 11th into the early morning hours of the 12th. The first of these was early in the morning on the 11th which brought approximately one to three inches of snow to the southern Texas Panhandle and northwestern South Plains. A lull in activity occurred during the afternoon before another upper level disturbance brought additional snow accumulations in the evening of the 11th through the early morning hours of the 12th.||Snowfall totals from NWS cooperative weather observers are listed below:||5.0 inches at Muleshoe (Bailey County), 4.5 inches at Morton (Cochran County), 4.5 inches at Friona (Parmer County), 4.0 inches at Hart (Castro County), 3.8 inches at Dimmitt (Castro County), 3.0 inches at Olton (Lamb County), 3.0 inches at Silverton (Briscoe County), 2.5 inches at Littlefield (Lamb County), 2.4 inches at Plainview (Hale County), 2.0 inches at Turkey (Hall County), 1.5 inches at Northfield (Motley County), 1.5 inches at Ropesville (Hockley County), 1.1 inches at Shallowater (Lubbock County), 1.0 inches at Levelland (Hockley County), and 1.0 inches at Matador (Motley County).

An upper level ridge of high pressure centered over the Southern Plains on July 10th, began to amplify and expand north-northeast into the Plains and Mississippi Valley through the middle portions of the month, resulting in increased subsidence and hence, hotter temperatures as readings climbed into the mid to upper 90s across much of the Ark-La-Tex. Very little mixing of dewpoints occurred during this time period, resulting in heat indices ranging from 105 to 110 degrees during the afternoons across much of East Texas through July 12th. However, a weak surface boundary became stationary on the 13th across portions of Northeast Texas, Southern Arkansas, and North Louisiana, which resulted in low level moisture pooling near and south of this boundary. Thus, even more oppressive heat indices were observed across more of East Texas on the 13th.

Temperatures exceeded 105 degrees across Atascosa, Bastrop, Bexar, Burnet, Caldwell, De Witt, Fayette, Gillespie, Gonzales, Guadalupe, Hays, Kerr, Lee, Llano, Medina, Travis, and Uvalde Counties. The temperature reached 110 in Luling and 109 in New Braunfels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Up-slope east to northeast flow associated with a weak frontal boundary across the area assisted in maintaining a moist and unstable atmosphere across the Texas Panhandle. Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon and evening as an upper level disturbance in northwest flow aloft. The strongly sheared environment led to rotating storms, which supported the production of very large hail and damaging winds across western, northern and central parts of the Panhandle. A brief landspout tornado developed in the southwest Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell 6 miles south-southwest of Texhoma.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-afternoon funnel cloud was sighted.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust was measured at the Easterwood Field Airport ASOS Site KCLL.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike took down a large, tall tree and caused some property damage in and around a residential home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hanna made landfall on the lower Texas coast but produced tropical storm force winds and elevated tides, minor coastal flooding across coastal Matagorda and Brazoria Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weatherflow sensors at Matagorda Bay and Surfside Beach measured sustained winds at tropical storm force for a period of time on July 25th. Minor coastal flooding and beach erosion were also observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of the fifth, scattered thunderstorms developed in eastern New Mexico ahead of an upper level shear axis. These storms became outflow dominant by the time they reached West Texas. A cluster of thunderstorms was able to produce a couple of severe wind gusts in Lubbock County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Texas Tech University West Texas mesonet site at Reese measured a wind gust to 68 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Below-average precipitation, which begin in April, continued through May and into June across portions of the Texas Panhandle. While pockets of around 2-3 inch rainfall occurred in June, many areas received less than an inch during what is typically one of the wetter months of the year. This led caused an area of severe drought (D2) that was initially categorized in the far northwest Texas Panhandle in May to spread south and east. Severe drought was declared in numerous additional Texas Panhandle counties in June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas of severe drought (D2) emerged during the second week of the month in the western and far southeast northwest parts of the county. By the third week, these areas had expanded to include all of the county, with extreme drought (D3) developing in the far northwest. Thunderstorms brought some relief to eastern sections, while extreme drought expanded to include roughly the western half of county by the end of the month, and severe drought continued across central and eastern sections.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper-level low pressure center moved east from southern Colorado across south central Kansas through the late afternoon on Tuesday, June 9, 2020. As it did, a surface low deepened and moved east across Kansas pushing a cold front south across the Oklahoma and Texas Panhandles before moving into western Texas. The cold front moved into the Oklahoma Panhandle early in the morning and quickly swept south through through the Texas Panhandle, continuing to move across the region. A strong pressure gradient between the low over Kansas and high pressure surging southeast out of Colorado created very strong winds across the Texas and Oklahoma Panhandles behind the front, which lasted into the early evening.||Across the northern half of the Texas Panhandle, sustained winds increased into the 40 to greater than 50 MPH range out of the north to northwest, with gusts mainly in the 55 to 65 MPH range. Farther south, sustained winds generally ranged from 37 to 46 MPH with gusts mainly in the 50 to 60 MPH range. These strong winds lasted most of the day before finally diminishing from west to east beginning in the late afternoon. Although the relative humidity was only 15 to 35 percent across the northwestern two-thirds of the Texas Panhandle, the high winds and dry fuels associated with recent onset of moderate to extreme drought in the area led to a high wildfire danger across part of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The JA Ranch West Texas Mesonet station 4 miles east of Palo Duro measured sustained winds in the 40 to 46 MPH range during this time period. The highest sustained wind of 46 MPH occurred at 1425CST.
