Major Hurricane Laura tracked north northwest across the Central and Northern Gulf of Mexico from the Central Carribean Sea near Cuba, making landfall in Southwest Louisiana near Cameron around 1 am on August 27th as a strong Category 4 hurricane with maximum sustained winds near 150 mph, and a minimum central pressure of 938 mb. Laura remained a hurricane as it tracked north across Southwest and Central Louisiana and into Natchitoches Parish, before weakening to a tropical storm as it moved northeast across Northcentral Louisiana and Southcentral Arkansas. Despite being on the west side of Laura's center, portions of extreme Eastern Texas observed wind gusts between 45-60+ mph, mainly across Sabine, San Augustine, Angelina, Shelby, Rusk, Panola, and Harrison Counties. These winds downed trees and power lines over these areas during the morning, before winds weakened in the afternoon with the departure of Laura into Southern Arkansas. Rainfall amounts ranged from 1.50-3.00 inches across the eastern border counties with Louisiana. Higher amounts of 3-6+ inches were observed across Southern Panola, Shelby, San Augustine, and Sabine Counties. However, no flooding was reported across East Texas from Hurricane Laura.||Here are the peak wind gusts at various automated stations across the affected areas of East Texas: ||Marshall (Harrison County), 48 mph|Carthage (Panola County), 47 mph|Sabine South RAWS (Sabine County), 44 mph |San Augustine (San Augustine County), 36 mph. ||Here are the known damage across various counties affected in East Texas: ||Angelina County: Scattered trees and power lines down countywide. ||San Augustine County: Scattered trees and power lines down countywide. ||Sabine County: Numerous trees down across the eastern half of the county with more sporadic damage across the western half of the county. The roof of a Ashley Furniture store collapsed in Hemphill. The damage otherwise was mostly uprooted trees and large broken branches, with trees falling through homes in the McGee's Landing area. A pine tree fell into a single wide mobile home and killed a 54 year old man in the Pendleton Harbor subdivision near Toledo Bend Reservoir. The tree fall was generally to the south-southwest to south-southeast. ||Shelby County: Scattered trees and power lines down countywide. ||Panola County: Sporadic trees down in Carthage and southwest of De Berry. ||Rusk County: Numerous trees and power lines down countywide. ||Harrison County: A tree was blown down onto a house in Waskom. Sporadic trees were also downed across various locations in the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observed scattered tree damage across the parish with more widespread damage across the southeastern corner of the parish and exposed areas to the open waters of Toledo Bend Reservoir in the far southwestern portion of the parish.  Damage included snapped trees, roof damage to an apartment complex, and clusters of uprooted trees.  Tree fall in the parish ranged from south to south-southeast. The damage survey estimated sustained winds were 50 to 60 mph with peak wind gusts in the 70 to 80 mph range in the southern portion of the county.  Sustained winds were estimated to be 40 to 50 with gusts to the 60 to 70 mph range in the northern portion of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front associated with an upper level disturbance passed through deep South Texas on the evening of August 1st into the overnight hours. Strong thunderstorms formed along and ahead of this front in conjunction with preexisting convective boundaries in the area, bringing damaging winds and flooding rain to the lower Rio Grande Valley. The impacts were primarily felt in Hidalgo and Cameron County, which were still recovering from ample rainfall and remnant flooding from Hurricane Hanna at the end of July. Standing water from Hurricane Hanna was still reported across western Cameron, southeast Hidalgo, and southwest Willacy with road closures still in place at the beginning of August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large metal awning from a local convenient gas station severely damaged and partially blown off. Wind gusts of at least 60 mph were estimated to have occurred at the intersection of Bass Blvd and Business 83 at 8:30 PM CDT when a strong thunderstorm moved through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front associated with an upper level disturbance passed through deep South Texas on the evening of August 1st into the overnight hours. Strong thunderstorms formed along and ahead of this front in conjunction with preexisting convective boundaries in the area, bringing damaging winds and flooding rain to the lower Rio Grande Valley. The impacts were primarily felt in Hidalgo and Cameron County, which were still recovering from ample rainfall and remnant flooding from Hurricane Hanna at the end of July. Standing water from Hurricane Hanna was still reported across western Cameron, southeast Hidalgo, and southwest Willacy with road closures still in place at the beginning of August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management continued to report road closures across portions of northwest Cameron County due to ongoing flooding from rainfall from Hurricane Hanna, along with additional rainfall at the end of July and then the rainfall on August 1st. Road closures from standing water were reported through the afternoon of August 8th, with gradual improvement over the course of the first week of August as no additional significant rainfall fell.  Note:  Estimated flood damage from this event is included in the Hanna episode as part of July 2020 Storm Data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance high instability and moderate wind shear led to severe storm development across the Texas Panhandle. The strongest storm produced a swath of thunderstorm wind damage around 2 miles wide and 4 miles long occurred along the Fritch Highway near Webb Rd. Damage within the area included numerous broken or leaning power poles, downed power lines and damage to roofs, porches, and awnings at local residences, especially those facing west. In addition, many large tree limbs in the area were snapped and some out outbuildings and old barns in the area were destroyed. One single wide trailer was pushed off its blocks. Damage was consistent with straight line winds. All while this was going some places in north Amarillo saw large hail up to 1.75 inches in diameter and even some areas of accumulating hail up to 5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind farm with 10 meter wind sensor reported 61 mph wind gust.

The subtropical mid level ridge of high pressure has continued near to well above normal temperatures and dry conditions across Deep South Texas and the Rio Grande Valley for most of November. Severe (D2) drought conditions developed across southern Zapata and southwestern Starr counties heading in to mid November.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The subtropical mid level ridge of high pressure has continued near to well above normal temperatures and dry conditions across Zapata County for most of November. This helped to develop Severe (D2) Drought conditions across the southwest corner of the county. These conditions persisted through the end of the month, before expanding into the beginning into December.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level disturbance was moving over West Texas. Surface winds were bringing upslope flow along the mountains. These conditions resulted in lift that produced a thunderstorm with strong winds in Presidio County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved across Presidio County and produced a 67 mph wind gust at the AWOS site near the town of Presidio.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved into West Texas. High pressure and cold air behind the cold front caused strong winds to develop in the Guadalupe and Delaware Mountains.
A strong cold front moved into West Texas. High pressure and cold air behind the cold front caused strong winds to develop in the Guadalupe and Delaware Mountains.

A tropical wave emerged off of Africa during August 16 and then developed into a depression by the 20th. The system gradually became better organized as it passed across the Greater Antilles. It entered the Gulf of Mexico and became a hurricane on the 25th. Outer bands spread inland during the afternoon of the 26th and it made landfall at Cameron, Louisiana at 1AM local time on the 27th. The west side of the category 4 hurricane impacted Southeast Texas as the centered moved north across west Louisiana and caused damages totaling roughly $1.95 Billion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a mandatory evacuation for the county. Many trees and power lines were blown down across the county. Homes and business were damaged from trees falling on them or wind. Over 40 percent of the county was without power immediately after the storm. Wind gusts ranged from 35 to 70 mph across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor placed a large portion of West Central Texas in a Severe Drought for the month of August.  A period of very hot temperatures and below normal rainfall amounts led to a flash drought across a large part of West Central Texas.  These hot temperatures resulted in numerous grass fires.   According to the Texas Crop Report, dryland cotton across the northern Edwards Plateau and northwest Hill Country continued to show signs of stress where moisture had been inadequate.  Also, some ranchers, in this region, had to provide supplemental feed to their livestock because of the lack of grasses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor placed all of McCulloch County into the severe drought category.

All while this was going some places in north Amarillo saw large hail up to 1.75 inches in diameter and even some areas of accumulating hail up to 5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roof blown off home and golf ball size hail.

Damage was consistent with straight line winds. All while this was going some places in north Amarillo saw large hail up to 1.75 inches in diameter and even some areas of accumulating hail up to 5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Travel trailer damaged...fences blown over...power lines blown down and metal sheds destroyed due to thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The subtropical mid level ridge of high pressure has continued near to well above normal temperatures and dry conditions across Deep South Texas and the Rio Grande Valley for most of November. Severe (D2) drought conditions developed across southern Zapata and southwestern Starr counties heading in to mid November.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The subtropical mid level ridge of high pressure has continued near to well above normal temperatures and dry conditions across Starr County for most of November. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The airmass over South Central Texas was very moist with high precipitable water. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical wave emerged off of Africa during August 16 and then developed into a depression by the 20th. The system gradually became better organized as it passed across the Greater Antilles. It entered the Gulf of Mexico and became a hurricane on the 25th. Outer bands spread inland during the afternoon of the 26th and it made landfall at Cameron, Louisiana at 1AM local time on the 27th. The west side of the category 4 hurricane impacted Southeast Texas as the centered moved north across west Louisiana and caused damages totaling roughly $1.95 Billion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a mandatory evacuation for the county. Many trees and power lines were blown down across the county. Homes and businesses were damaged from trees falling on them or wind. Over 40 percent of the county was without power immediately after the storm. Wind gusts ranged from 35 to 70 mph across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor placed a large portion of West Central Texas in a Severe Drought for the month of August.  A period of very hot temperatures and below normal rainfall amounts led to a flash drought across a large part of West Central Texas.  These hot temperatures resulted in numerous grass fires.   According to the Texas Crop Report, dryland cotton across the northern Edwards Plateau and northwest Hill Country continued to show signs of stress where moisture had been inadequate.  Also, some ranchers, in this region, had to provide supplemental feed to their livestock because of the lack of grasses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor placed a large part of Crockett County into the severe drought category, while the southern part of the Crockett County was placed in extreme drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge of high pressure centered over New Mexico into Western Texas and Oklahoma expanded east into East Texas and Western Louisiana on August 13th. This strong sinking motion beneath this ridge resulted in compressional heating over these areas, with afternoon temperatures climbing into the upper 90s to in excess of 100 degrees. Little mixing of dewpoints occurred, with the heat and humidity contributing to heat indices ranging from 105-110 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and humid conditions continued from the final day in August through the first couple days of September, with temperatures climbing into the mid and upper 90s across Deep East Texas. Oppressively high dewpoints in the mid to upper 70s in wake of a warm frontal passage on August 31st shifted back north across much of East Texas and North Louisiana, which when combined with the very hot temperatures, resulted in heat indices ranging from 105-110 degrees across these areas. This was brief though before areas of showers and thunderstorms ahead of a closed upper low pressure system over the Four Corners Region affected Deep East Texas on the 3rd, resulting in much cooler temperatures.

Extreme to exceptional drought conditions continued, especially in the southwest Texas Panhandle and western Oklahoma Panhandle. Severe drought was prominent in the south to southeastern Texas Panhandle. During the month of November little rainfall occurred, but one rain event was able to improve conditions in the northeastern combined Panhandles, primarily in Beaver County Oklahoma. Winter wheat got a decent start with the late October snow. However, dry conditions through November quickly made for poor to good conditions with dryland crops struggling the most. Ranchers continue to bring in supplemental feed due to poor range lands with very little grass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Deaf Smith County had seen some improvements during the last days of October. With November seeing a monthly defect of around half an inch of rain. Areas in the southern half of the county that were in extreme drought (D3) to exceptional drought (D4) saw small expansions by the third week of November. Much of the northern half to third of the county remained in severe drought (D2) by the end of the month. The county still remains in a 8 to 12 inch rainfall deficit year to date. Pasture and unirrigated crops remained in very poor condition.

Winter wheat got a decent start with the late October snow. However, dry conditions through November quickly made for poor to good conditions with dryland crops struggling the most. Ranchers continue to bring in supplemental feed due to poor range lands with very little grass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roughly 80 percent of Randall County started the month of November in Severe Drought (D2). Conditions quickly returned to extreme drought (D3) in the south to southwest portion of the county given little rainfall in November. The county remained in an 8 to 12 inch rainfall deficit year to date by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Laura made landfall along the southwest Louisiana coast. The hurricane brought high surf, elevated tides and some winds and wind gusts to tropical storm force along the SE Texas coast. There we no measured sustained winds to tropical storm force at land observation sites and very little rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Period of storm surge flooding and high surf from 8/26 to 8/27 leading to flooding of low lying areas and roads. Beach erosion on Gulf side. Tide gauges measured tides 3 to 4 feet above MHHW. Bolivar Peninsula and Galveston Island, including Jamaica Beach, with multiple roads closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and humid conditions continued from the final day in August through the first day of September, with temperatures climbing into the mid and upper 90s across much of East Texas. Oppressively high dewpoints in the mid to upper 70s in wake of a warm frontal passage on August 31st shifted back north across much of East Texas and North Louisiana, which when combined with the very hot temperatures, resulted in heat indices ranging from 105-110 degrees across these areas. This was brief though before areas of showers and thunderstorms ahead of a closed upper low pressure system over the Four Corners Region affected much of East Texas on the 2nd, resulting in much cooler temperatures.

Hurricane Laura made landfall along the southwest Louisiana coast. The hurricane brought high surf, elevated tides and some winds and wind gusts to tropical storm force along the SE Texas coast. There we no measured sustained winds to tropical storm force at land observation sites and very little rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Period of storm surge flooding and high surf from 8/26 to 8/27 leading to flooding of low lying areas and roads around parts of Kemah and other coastal communities. Beach erosion. Tide gauges measured tides 3 to 4 feet above MHHW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor placed a large portion of West Central Texas in a Severe Drought for the month of August.  A period of very hot temperatures and below normal rainfall amounts led to a flash drought across a large part of West Central Texas.  These hot temperatures resulted in numerous grass fires.   According to the Texas Crop Report, dryland cotton across the northern Edwards Plateau and northwest Hill Country continued to show signs of stress where moisture had been inadequate.  Also, some ranchers, in this region, had to provide supplemental feed to their livestock because of the lack of grasses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor placed the eastern part of Menard County into the severe drought category.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge of high pressure centered over New Mexico into Western Texas and Oklahoma expanded east into East Texas and Western Louisiana on August 13th. This strong sinking motion beneath this ridge resulted in compressional heating over these areas, with afternoon temperatures climbing into the upper 90s to in excess of 100 degrees. Little mixing of dewpoints occurred, with the heat and humidity contributing to heat indices ranging from 105-110 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As Hurricane Laura traversed northward across the Gulf of Mexico in late August, outgoing swell propagated into the lower Texas coastal waters on August 27th. This swell was powerful enough to push water into the dunes across South Padre Island around high tide, which occurred at 2:36 AM CDT. The highest level that was reported was 1.87 feet above Mean Higher-High Water (MHHW; or the level above normally dry ground at the shoreline)  at 3:06 AM CDT, but levels remained above 1.25 feet MHHW from 11 PM CDT on August 26th until noon on August 27th. This activity was enough to cause some coastal erosion.||Farther up the coast, similar wave action and minor coastal flooding was likely - but there were no comparative measurements based on MHHW.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As swell from Hurricane Laura reached the lower Texas coast late on August 26th, the wave energy from this swell combined with high tide at 2:36 AM CDT on the 27th, allowed water levels to remain above 1.25 feet MHHW at the Brazos Santiago NDBC buoy from 11 PM on the 26th through noon on the 27th. The peak water level of 1.87 feet MHHW occurred at 3:06 AM CDT on the 27th. This activity caused some coastal erosion along the beaches of South Padre Island as high waves (up to 10+ feet in some cases) set-up and ran up into gaps in the dunes, into the dunes, or calved more robust dunes by erosion. Erosion began several hours prior to high tide due to wave action as Laura propagated its peak swell four to six hours prior to landfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As Hurricane Laura traversed northward across the Gulf of Mexico in late August, outgoing swell propagated into the lower Texas coastal waters on August 27th. This swell was powerful enough to push water into the dunes across South Padre Island around high tide, which occurred at 2:36 AM CDT. The highest level that was reported was 1.87 feet above Mean Higher-High Water (MHHW; or the level above normally dry ground at the shoreline)  at 3:06 AM CDT, but levels remained above 1.25 feet MHHW from 11 PM CDT on August 26th until noon on August 27th. This activity was enough to cause some coastal erosion.||Farther up the coast, similar wave action and minor coastal flooding was likely - but there were no comparative measurements based on MHHW.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As swell from Hurricane Laura reached the lower Texas coast late on August 26th, the wave energy from this swell combined with high tide (Port Mansfield Channel Entrance) at approximately 2:11 AM CDT  on the 27th, likely produced minor coastal flooding in the form of run-up to and just into the dunes at narrow beaches of Willacy County, both south and north of the Mansfield cut. Unknown is the degree of erosion; assumed is erosion similar to what was observed at South Padre Island. A peak water level of 1.8 feet above Mean Higher-High Water (MHHW, or above normally dry ground on the shoreline) occurred at 10:12 AM CST on the 27th at the Realitos Peninsula tide guage, inshore (Laguna Madre) about 11 miles southwest of the Willacy County barrier island line.

On the afternoon of the first, isolated but strong thunderstorms developed along a surface pressure trough located in the south-central Texas Panhandle. One of these storms was able to take advantage of the abundant instability and become very intense. However, a very dry sub-cloud layer allowed this storm to generate a severe wind gust near Lesley (Hall County). Additionally, local broadcast media relayed video of a tornado that lasted for a few seconds over open land in Hall County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Texas Tech University West Texas mesonet site near Lesley measured a wind gust to 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of August 9th the western combined Oklahoma and Texas Panhandle experienced scattered thunderstorms. The atmosphere was unstable enough to create large hail and damaging winds with some storms. There was also a line of storms that moved from west to east through the western Texas Panhandle that led to no reports of severe weather. The Oklahoma Panhandle had thunderstorms that were a bit more discrete and organized and these created some large hail up to the size of nickels with a few stones up to an inch reported. A couple of automated weather stations reported wind gusts up to 60 mph as well. There was one report of nickel size hail in the western Texas Panhandle early on in the event.

On August 14th, 2020, a few monsoon thunderstorms developed during peak heating of the late afternoon between 5 and 6 PM local time. A couple of these storms produce hail between the size of nickels to the size of a half dollar. The more impactful storms came later in the evening as a Mesoscale Convective Complex associated with an upper level disturbance moved around high pressure centered across the Desert Southwest and entered the central Oklahoma Panhandle from southeast Kansas. This complex created wind gusts of 65-70 mph which damaged trees in the Oklahoma Panhandle and provided additional minor damage into the north-central Texas Panhandle.

A line of storms moved through the region in the evening hours. The storms intensified as they reached the I-35 corridor, and a QLCS tornado occurred in Arlington. The tornado was rated an EF-2 because of roof damage to an apartment complex. Five injuries occurred along the tornado path, but none were life threatening. Notable wind damage was also reported across Fannin County from the line of storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An emergency manager reported a roof was blown off of a mobile home near Trenton. Power lines were also knocked down in Trenton. Time was estimated via radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of storms moved through the region in the evening hours. The storms intensified as they reached the I-35 corridor, and a QLCS tornado occurred in Arlington. The tornado was rated an EF-2 because of roof damage to an apartment complex. Five injuries occurred along the tornado path, but none were life threatening. Notable wind damage was also reported across Fannin County from the line of storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-2 tornado moved through Arlington producing mostly EF-0 and EF-1 damage, but a small area of EF-2 damage was found. The tornado began near West Mayfield Road and South Bowen Road where fence, shed and tree damage was found. The tornado traveled northeast from this location causing minor tree damage. Significant roof damage to several structures was noted along Colorado Lane. The tornado then crossed South Cooper Street and damaged a Burger Box fast food restaurant, Safelite AutoGlass store, and several warehouses in the area. The tornado damage in his location and along Colorado Lane was at EF-1 intensity. The tornado continued along an east-northeast track where it soon impacted the Waterdance and Mirage Apartments. Considerable roof damage was noted at these complexes, but the most significant damage occurred at the Waterdance Apartment complex. Large sections of the roof were ripped off of two buildings, resulting in an EF-2 rating at this location. From the apartment complex, the tornado weakened and traveled northeast for a few more miles before dissipating near East Park Row Drive and Carter Drive. Sporadic tree, siding, fence, and shingle damage was noted to the end of the path. The maximum winds with this tornado were around 115 mph. Five people were injured in this tornado, but none of the injuries were life threatening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front triggered scattered thunderstorms just north of San Angelo shortly after midnight.  These storms produced a strong outflow boundary with embedded multiple damaging thunderstorm wet microbursts that tracked across San Angelo.  This very strong thunderstorm outflow and wet microbursts resulted in extensive damage across portions of the city.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Weather Service conducted a storm survey across San Angelo.  A scattered line of thunderstorm produced a damaging thunderstorm outflow and multiple wet microbursts across the city.  This caused damaging straight line winds of 60 to 95 mph across the City of San Angelo, knocking down power poles and power lines, resulting in widespread power outages.  Numerous large tree limbs and some trees were uprooted. A wind gust of 86 mph was measured at the National Weather Service at 201 AM CDT.  The downburst was 10 miles long and about 2500 yards wide as it began about 2 miles northeast of San Angelo and continued for 8 miles southwest of San Angelo.||Radar and photographs clearly showed intense damaging thunderstorm downbursts impacting the city.  One impacted the north side near Chadbourne and Loop 306 where it damaged a gasoline station canopy, it knocked over a large power line that crossed Loop 306 and snapped a power pole. It also knocked down numerous large tree limbs and damaged some roofs in the area.  The severe wet microburst also damaged the Wool Hair building in the vicinity.  ||The downburst wind knocked down three power poles and power lines at Knickerbocker and Johnson Street.  The storm ripped off roofing material at the nearby Bellagio Apartments.  The damaging winds damaged the wall of a nearby commercial building.  The high winds also knocked down numerous tree limbs, broke windows and damaged roofs in the College Hills area.  ||The downburst winds removed shingles, knocked down signs, and numerous tree limbs on the southwest side of San Angelo near Lamar Elementary School.  As the downburst struck the KOA RV park near Lake Nasworthy, it knocked down numerous tree limbs and uprooted a few trees.  One Recreational Vehicle was knocked over on it's side.||Special thanks to the San Angelo Police Department, the San Angelo Fire Department,|San Angelo Emergency Management, San Angelo REACT and local residents and business owners for their excellent assistance.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front triggered scattered thunderstorms just north of San Angelo shortly after midnight.  These storms produced a strong outflow boundary with embedded multiple damaging thunderstorm wet microbursts that tracked across San Angelo.  This very strong thunderstorm outflow and wet microbursts resulted in extensive damage across portions of the city.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS Station at Mathis Airport in San Angelo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance interacted with a frontal boundary and an unstable airmass to produce a few severe thunderstorms across the region.  These storms resulted in a few severe thunderstorm wind gusts near Rule and Mertzon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An outbuilding and a chicken coop were destroyed by the damaging thunderstorm winds. The public estimated 60 to 70 mph wind gusts.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to two inch tree limbs were broken off by the thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of showers and thunderstorms developed across Northeast and East Texas during the afternoon through the overnight hours of August 16th-17th ahead of a weak cold front that moved south through the area. The hot and moist air ahead of the front generated a very unstable environment, allowing several of these storms to become severe during the afternoon through the early morning hours. These storms produced large hail, with isolated instances of wind damage in the form of downed trees, before gradually weakening during the early morning hours on the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell south of the Hagansport community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On August 14th, 2020, a few monsoon thunderstorms developed during peak heating of the late afternoon between 5 and 6 PM local time. A couple of these storms produce hail between the size of nickels to the size of a half dollar. The more impactful storms came later in the evening as a Mesoscale Convective Complex associated with an upper level disturbance moved around high pressure centered across the Desert Southwest and entered the central Oklahoma Panhandle from southeast Kansas. This complex created wind gusts of 65-70 mph which damaged trees in the Oklahoma Panhandle and provided additional minor damage into the north-central Texas Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports from local police of several downed power lines a few miles west of Spearman around the 15/207 area and a few miles north of Gruver on HWY 136. Additionally, several downed tree branches as well as loose objects, such as trampolines, had been displaced in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of storms moved through the region in the evening hours. The storms intensified as they reached the I-35 corridor, and a QLCS tornado occurred in Arlington. The tornado was rated an EF-2 because of roof damage to an apartment complex. Five injuries occurred along the tornado path, but none were life threatening. Notable wind damage was also reported across Fannin County from the line of storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A barn was damaged and a calf stable was destroyed along CR 1552 in Bailey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Major Hurricane Laura tracked north northwest across the Central and Northern Gulf of Mexico from the Central Carribean Sea near Cuba, making landfall in Southwest Louisiana near Cameron around 1 am on August 27th as a strong Category 4 hurricane with maximum sustained winds near 150 mph, and a minimum central pressure of 938 mb. Laura remained a hurricane as it tracked north across Southwest and Central Louisiana and into Natchitoches Parish, before weakening to a tropical storm as it moved northeast across Northcentral Louisiana and Southcentral Arkansas. Despite being on the west side of Laura's center, portions of extreme Eastern Texas observed wind gusts between 45-60+ mph, mainly across Sabine, San Augustine, Angelina, Shelby, Rusk, Panola, and Harrison Counties. These winds downed trees and power lines over these areas during the morning, before winds weakened in the afternoon with the departure of Laura into Southern Arkansas. Rainfall amounts ranged from 1.50-3.00 inches across the eastern border counties with Louisiana. Higher amounts of 3-6+ inches were observed across Southern Panola, Shelby, San Augustine, and Sabine Counties. However, no flooding was reported across East Texas from Hurricane Laura.||Here are the peak wind gusts at various automated stations across the affected areas of East Texas: ||Marshall (Harrison County), 48 mph|Carthage (Panola County), 47 mph|Sabine South RAWS (Sabine County), 44 mph |San Augustine (San Augustine County), 36 mph. ||Here are the known damage across various counties affected in East Texas: ||Angelina County: Scattered trees and power lines down countywide. ||San Augustine County: Scattered trees and power lines down countywide. ||Sabine County: Numerous trees down across the eastern half of the county with more sporadic damage across the western half of the county. The roof of a Ashley Furniture store collapsed in Hemphill. The damage otherwise was mostly uprooted trees and large broken branches, with trees falling through homes in the McGee's Landing area. A pine tree fell into a single wide mobile home and killed a 54 year old man in the Pendleton Harbor subdivision near Toledo Bend Reservoir. The tree fall was generally to the south-southwest to south-southeast. ||Shelby County: Scattered trees and power lines down countywide. ||Panola County: Sporadic trees down in Carthage and southwest of De Berry. ||Rusk County: Numerous trees and power lines down countywide. ||Harrison County: A tree was blown down onto a house in Waskom. Sporadic trees were also downed across various locations in the county.

August 12-16th was the hottest period of the year with an extended Heat Advisory and/or Excessive Heat Warning over the region. Actual temperatures soared to around 101-110 degrees in several places with heat index values ranging from 100-115 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: August 12-16 was the hottest period of the year with an extended Heat Advisory and/or Excessive Heat Warning for Hood County. Afternoon Heat Index Values ranged from 105 to 115 degrees. The ambient temperatures topped out between 105-110 degrees during this period. This county was upgraded to an Excessive Heat Warning on August 13th through the 15th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of showers and thunderstorms developed across Northeast and East Texas during the afternoon through the overnight hours of August 16th-17th ahead of a weak cold front that moved south through the area. The hot and moist air ahead of the front generated a very unstable environment, allowing several of these storms to become severe during the afternoon through the early morning hours. These storms produced large hail, with isolated instances of wind damage in the form of downed trees, before gradually weakening during the early morning hours on the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail dollar size hail fell between Lake Fork and Lake Quitman.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stout mid level ridge built over Deep South Texas during the last few days of August, allowing high temperatures to climb. These high temperatures combined with high surface relative humidity, allowed afternoon heat indices to climb to a dangerous 111 degrees or greater for a couple of hours in the afternoons of August 30th and 31st in populated areas along the Rio Grande.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices around McAllen, TX exceeded 111 degrees for a couple of hours starting at 1:50 PM CDT and the MFE ASOS even exceeded 116 degrees for a period of time around 4 PM CDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stout mid level ridge built over Deep South Texas during the last few days of August, allowing high temperatures to climb. These high temperatures combined with high surface relative humidity, allowed afternoon heat indices to climb to a dangerous 111 degrees or greater for a couple of hours in the afternoons of August 30th and 31st in populated areas along the Rio Grande.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices in Cameron County exceeded 111 degrees for a couple of hours starting at 1:50 PM CDT and the BRO ASOS even exceeded 114 degrees for a period of time around 3 PM CDT, and HRL topped at 112 degrees around 2 PM CDT.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices around Zapata, TX exceeded 111 degrees for a couple of hours starting around 4 PM CDT and the APY ASOS even exceeded 115 degrees for a period of time around 5 PM CDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon/early evening of August 10th, 2020, scattered severe thunderstorms created damage across the combined Oklahoma and Texas Panhandles. These storms eventually congealed into a line as they exited the eastern Panhandles. Several reports of hail between the size of Pennies and Quarters were reported across the area and a few storms produced up to golf ball size hail. Thunderstorm wind damage was reported along with a network of weather equipment measuring wind gusts near 70 MPH. Some of the more significant damage included a couple trailers which were completed totaled near Perryton, TX as well as 2 miles of power poles downed near Spearman, TX. In eastern Oklahoma Panhandle there was also some rural flooding reported near Knowles, OK from heavy rains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm spotter measured ping pong ball size hail along US Highway 54 southwest of Stratford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm developed and moved along a frontal boundary south of Miami, Texas on the afternoon of August 16th. The presence of the boundary and high instability aided the storm in developing a strong circulation and produced a short lived tornado which was on the ground for several minutes and less than two miles. The tornado did minor damage to a residence and power lines in the area. The storm also produced large hail across portions of the area.

Isolated to scattered thunderstorms produced a severe microburst winds near Baird and Menard on August 16.  A cluster of thunderstorms produced a severe thunderstorm outflow that resulted in a 59 mph wind gust at the Texas Tech Mesonet Station near Clyde on August 16.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Menard County Sheriff reported a tree blown down in Menard.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report of telephone poles bent over and tree limbs on the roadway on U.S. Highway 283 north of Baird.  The time of this report was estimated by radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Texas Tech West Texas Mesonet Station located 2 miles northwest of Menard measured a thunderstorm wind gust of 64 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Texas Tech West Texas Mesonet near Clyde measured a thunderstorm outflow wind gust to 59 mph.  There was no thunderstorm occurring during the time of the gust.  The storms were well to the north of Clyde.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of showers and thunderstorms developed across Northeast and East Texas during the afternoon through the overnight hours of August 16th-17th ahead of a weak cold front that moved south through the area. The hot and moist air ahead of the front generated a very unstable environment, allowing several of these storms to become severe during the afternoon through the early morning hours. These storms produced large hail, with isolated instances of wind damage in the form of downed trees, before gradually weakening during the early morning hours on the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down near the intersection of Highway 7 and County Road 761.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stout mid level ridge built over Deep South Texas during the last few days of August, allowing high temperatures to climb. These high temperatures combined with high surface relative humidity, allowed afternoon heat indices to climb to a dangerous 111 degrees or greater for a couple of hours in the afternoons of August 30th and 31st in populated areas along the Rio Grande.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices around McAllen, TX exceeded 111 degrees for a couple of hours starting at 1:50 PM CDT and hovered around 112 degrees for a couple of hours during the afternoon of August 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As Hurricane Laura traversed northward across the Gulf of Mexico in late August, outgoing swell propagated into the lower Texas coastal waters on August 27th. This swell was powerful enough to push water into the dunes across South Padre Island around high tide, which occurred at 2:36 AM CDT. The highest level that was reported was 1.87 feet above Mean Higher-High Water (MHHW; or the level above normally dry ground at the shoreline)  at 3:06 AM CDT, but levels remained above 1.25 feet MHHW from 11 PM CDT on August 26th until noon on August 27th. This activity was enough to cause some coastal erosion.||Farther up the coast, similar wave action and minor coastal flooding was likely - but there were no comparative measurements based on MHHW.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As swell from Hurricane Laura reached the lower Texas coast late on August 26th and early on August 27th, the wave energy from this swell combined with high tide (Port Mansfield Channel Entrance and Malaquite Beach) at approximately 2:11 AM CDT  on the 27th, likely produced minor coastal flooding in the form of run-up to and just into the dunes at narrow beaches of Willacy County, both south and north of the Mansfield cut. Unknown is the degree of erosion; assumed is erosion similar to what was observed at South Padre Island. Unfortunately, due to heavy erosion from Hurricane Hanna a month earlier, as well as the difficult-to-reach location on Padre Island National Seashore, it is unknown how much additional erosion occurred along the Kenedy County barrier island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon/early evening of August 10th, 2020, scattered severe thunderstorms created damage across the combined Oklahoma and Texas Panhandles. These storms eventually congealed into a line as they exited the eastern Panhandles. Several reports of hail between the size of Pennies and Quarters were reported across the area and a few storms produced up to golf ball size hail. Thunderstorm wind damage was reported along with a network of weather equipment measuring wind gusts near 70 MPH. Some of the more significant damage included a couple trailers which were completed totaled near Perryton, TX as well as 2 miles of power poles downed near Spearman, TX. In eastern Oklahoma Panhandle there was also some rural flooding reported near Knowles, OK from heavy rains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported dime size hail in Gruver, TX.

A supercell thunderstorm developed and moved along a frontal boundary south of Miami, Texas on the afternoon of August 16th. The presence of the boundary and high instability aided the storm in developing a strong circulation and produced a short lived tornado which was on the ground for several minutes and less than two miles. The tornado did minor damage to a residence and power lines in the area. The storm also produced large hail across portions of the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado did minor damage around a residence which included,|very minor damage to the home roof cap, a rolled and displaced air|conditioner unit, and a destroyed free standing antenna tower. A|heavy duty metal storage box with a stored car inside was also|rolled onto some farming equipment. The tornado stripped|significant amounts of sheet metal off two barns and caused one|wall to collapse. The EF1 rating was due to four 10 inch power|poles which were snapped where the tornado crossed a nearby |county road. Debris from objects around the home and sheet metal |from the barns were carried 100-200 yards down the path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of the 15th of August, monsoon moisture and a weak upper level disturbance contributed to development of a couple thunderstorms that developed up over the east-central Texas Panhandle and eventually congealed into one before dissipating. Winds aloft were weak and could not steer the storms very far before they dissipated. There was just enough instability and wind shear that one storm produce hail up to 1.75 inches in diameter just northeast of Panhandle, Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A motorist drove into golf ball size hail along Highway 60 northeast of Panhandle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of the 28th of August a severe thunderstorm formed on the New Mexico/Texas State Line near Nera Visa, NM as a line of thunderstorms approached from the west. These thunderstorms did not maintain or gain strength as they entered the Texas Panhandle. However the thunderstorm that formed out ahead of the line on the state line managed to gain enough strength to put out a wind gust of 58 MPH. The line of thunderstorms did come into the northwest combined Oklahoma/Texas Panhandles but quickly became less organized with no severe characteristics.

Many areas across the combined Oklahoma and Texas Panhandles saw improvements in drought going into August. A couple of rain events help keep those areas from degradation during the month with some even improving by the end of the month. Northwest Cimarron County in Oklahoma was in extreme drought (D3) for the longest time until the end of the month after the August 29th event. An area in extreme drought in east central Texas Panhandle managed to leave the extreme drought category as well. However, the southwest Texas Panhandle kept missing out on rain with all storms passing to the north and northwest. Areas in severe drought and extreme drought are looking at a 6 to 12 inches deficit for rainfall year today. Many ranchers are either selling their cattle or buying feed as there are no rangeland grasses. Many dryland crops have failed thus far, even with the July and August rains that came just too late.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A portion of the southwest corner of the county was in severe drought going into August. About mid-August a small area of extreme drought conditions started showing as in the southwest. One rain event at the end of August briefly improved conditions shrinking  areas of severe and extrem drought just slightly. Soil moisture has been classified and very poor to fare. Agricultural impacts have not improved through the month of August. Rain deficits remain between 6 to 12 inches year to date.  Hay prices in the TX PH were $10 to $20 higher per ton compared to the rest of the state. Range land grasses are sparse making it pricy to feed cattle. A lot of dry land crops were abandoned and non-irrigated crops did not grow.

The rest of the county was abnormally dry to moderate drought (D1). Carson County managed to receive some beneficial rainfall, around 2 to 3 inches, across much of the county allowing all D2 and D3 areas to be removed by August 29th.  Hay prices in the TX PH were $10 to $20 higher per ton compared to the rest of the state. A lot of dry land crops were abandoned and non-irrigated crops did not grow. Monsoonal rains in the latter part of July and early August helped soil moisture but it was too late for almost all dry land crops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance high instability and moderate wind shear led to severe storm development across the Texas Panhandle. The strongest storm produced a swath of thunderstorm wind damage around 2 miles wide and 4 miles long occurred along the Fritch Highway near Webb Rd. Damage within the area included numerous broken or leaning power poles, downed power lines and damage to roofs, porches, and awnings at local residences, especially those facing west. In addition, many large tree limbs in the area were snapped and some out outbuildings and old barns in the area were destroyed. One single wide trailer was pushed off its blocks. Damage was consistent with straight line winds. All while this was going some places in north Amarillo saw large hail up to 1.75 inches in diameter and even some areas of accumulating hail up to 5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding in west Amarillo on Coulter St near Teddy Jacks restaurant.

Almost all of the August rain events missed Deaf Smith and Randall Counties to the north and northwest. Conditions are so bad farmers are not going to be able to plant winter wheat.  About 16,200 acres of non-irrigated cotton crop as perished due to the drought. Corn has had a 10% yield reduction from last year due to the extreme dry conditions during pollination.

Extreme to exceptional drought conditions continued, especially in the southwest Texas Panhandle and western Oklahoma Panhandle. Severe drought was prominent in the south to southeastern Texas Panhandle. During the month of November little rainfall occurred, but one rain event was able to improve conditions in the northeastern combined Panhandles, primarily in Beaver County Oklahoma. Winter wheat got a decent start with the late October snow. However, dry conditions through November quickly made for poor to good conditions with dryland crops struggling the most. Ranchers continue to bring in supplemental feed due to poor range lands with very little grass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Going into the month of November only a small area of severe drought (D2) existed in north to northwest Gray County. With an already 6 to 12 inch annual deficit that increased by up to an additional inch, around 90 percent of the county was included in severe drought category by the end of the month.

Areas in severe drought and extreme drought are looking at a 6 to 12 inches deficit for rainfall year today. Many ranchers are either selling their cattle or buying feed as there are no rangeland grasses. Many dryland crops have failed thus far, even with the July and August rains that came just too late.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The northern half of Gray County started August out in Extreme Drought (D3), with a small area of Severe Drought (D2) separating the southern half which was in Moderate Drought (D1) in the beginning of the month.  Much of the county was still 1 to 2 inches below average for the month, even with some areas receiving up to 2 inches. Part of this 2 inches that fell, fell at the end of the month, allowing for the Extreme Drought to be reduced to Severe Drought. But the vast majority of the county still ended in the month in Extreme Drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak surface boundary served as a focus for thunderstorm development during the afternoon of the 16th across southern Oklahoma and western north Texas with numerous severe wind and hail reports were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roof blown off structure on main street in Seymour. Several tree limbs downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusual strong Canadian Cold Front and an upper level low parked across the Four Corners resulted in very heavy rainfall in a short period time and a few severe weather reports in portions of the northern Big Country.  This heavy rain flooded low water crossings and even U.S. 277 across the region and caused Lake Stamford to flood.  Two occupants in a vehicle went missing on September 9.  They both drowned as they attempted to cross a flooded low water crossing on Red Mud Creek in Jones County.  One body was recovered on September 10 and the other was recovered on September 16.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Texas Tech West Texas Mesonet located just northwest of Haskell measured a wind gust to 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The airmass over South Central Texas was very moist with high precipitable water. A weakness developed in the ridge in the mid-levels of the atmosphere above this moist boundary layer. This allowed thunderstorms to develop and some of these storms produced heavy rain that led to flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain that led to flash flooding. ATXFloods reported that Onion Creek ran out of its banks closing Garrison Rd. in Buda.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough moved very slowly from the Rockies into the Plains during the first week of September, resulting in several days of showers and thunderstorms. Many storms became strong with gusty winds and hail, but the primary result was heavy rain and flooding. Unfortunately, a couple died in Temple Texas in a house fire that was started by lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Grayson County Sheriff's Office reported that roads west of the city of Sherman along Highway 82 were under water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Beta began as a low pressure area over the southwest Gulf of Mexico on September 16th. This system organized into a tropical depression on the 17th as it moved slowly northward. It became Tropical Storm Beta on the 18th while moving north into the northwest Gulf of Mexico. It became stationary on the 19th as steering flow collapsed. Beta moved on a slow and steady course to the west-northwest toward the Middle Texas coast on the 20th and 21st and made landfall on the southern end of the Matagorda Peninsula late on the evening of 21st. With the slow movement of Beta and higher astronomical tides, a long duration coastal flood event occurred from the 19th to early on the 23rd, with 2 to 3.5 feet of inundation occurring along the Middle Texas coast. The high tides and surge flooded several roads in Corpus Christi, Aransas Pass, Rockport, Fulton, Port O'Connor, Indianola, and Port Lavaca. Locally heavy rainfall occurred during the early morning hours of the 22nd over eastern Victoria County that produced flash flooding in Inez.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tide levels ranged from 2 to 3 feet of inundation during the period. Flooding occurred on Harbor Island Road just west of the ferry landing on Highway 361. Nearly a foot of water covered portions of Water Street in Rockport. Several inches of water made it into carports along Copano Ridge Drive. Water overflowed Fulton harbor onto nearby roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms impacted the combined Texas and Oklahoma Panhandles. Many of these storms were initiated along outflow boundaries produced by storms that developed during the peak heating of the afternoon. Moisture was plentiful in the lower levels of the atmosphere with just enough instability for some of these storms to produce wind gusts up to and exceeding 60 mph along with penny to quarter size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While many places improved in drought conditions leading up to September, a several counties across the Texas and Oklahoma Panhandle continued to experience severe drought (D2) conditions while a small area in the southwest Texas Panhandle remained in extreme drought (D3) status. Only one day of rain occurred in September, however it was enough to keep some areas from degrading with regard to drought status. The most common impacts were limited range land grasses for ranchers and lost crops to farmers. Also, some farmers were not able to start planting winter wheat at the end of the month due to the lack of soil moisture.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The western to southwest one third of the county was in severe drought (D2) through the entire month. There was a small portion of the southwest corner of the county that was in extreme drought (D3) going into the first of the month. This area of D3 quickly improved to D2 with beneficial rains that fell on the first of the month.  No other precipitation fell the rest of the month with D3 conditions creeping back into the southwest corner by the end of the month.  The county still remained in a 6 -12 rain deficit year to date by the end of the month.  Ranchers are still having to sell cattle or buy feed with range land grasses limited to non-existent.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While many places improved in drought conditions leading up to September, a several counties across the Texas and Oklahoma Panhandle continued to experience severe drought (D2) conditions while a small area in the southwest Texas Panhandle remained in extreme drought (D3) status. The most common impacts were limited range land grasses for ranchers and lost crops to farmers. Also, some farmers were not able to start planting winter wheat at the end of the month due to the lack of soil moisture.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Collingsworth County started out in with moderate drought (D1) in the west and northern part of the county, while almost half of the county in the east and southeast saw severe drought (D2) conditions. Thunderstorms on September 1st improved some of this area of severe drought but still left a good portion of the southeast corner in D2 status. The county remained in a 6 to 12 inch deficit year to date by the end of the month leaving some ranchers and farmers dealing with agricultural impacts such as shortage of range land grasses for cattle and dry soil conditions for crops.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Randall County was in extreme drought (D3) across the southern two thirds of the county going into September. The northern third was in severe drought conditions (D2). Conditions continued to worsen with less than a tenth of inch of rain on September 1st and no rain occurred for the rest of the month. By the end of September, a small sliver of the county in the far southwest degraded into exceptional drought (D4).  Conditions were so bad in September that farmers could not begin to plant winter wheat and were waiting for some soil moisture to return before planting could begin. Soil moisture was rated very poor to poor through the month of September. Some wells around canyon were reportedly going dry. Lake Tanglewood was reported to be at it's lowest level since the 2011-2012 drought and some boat lifts and piers were no longer usable. Ranchers are still having to sell cattle or buy feed with range land grasses non-existent. The county remained in a 6-12 rain deficit year to date by the end of the month.

A stout mid level ridge built over Deep South Texas during the last few days of August and into the beginning of September, allowing high temperatures to climb. These high temperatures combined with high surface relative humidity, allowed afternoon heat indices to climb to a dangerous 111 degrees or greater for a couple of hours in the afternoon of September 1st in populated areas of the Rio Grande Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High afternoon temperatures combined with high relative humidity values to create heat indices that exceeded 111 degrees Fahrenheit for a couple of hours in Brownsville, Harlingen, and the surrounding areas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gray County started the month of September with a majority of the county in severe drought (D2). The exception to this was the far west and far southern part of the county that was only in moderate drought (D1). Gray County received a quarter to three quarters of an inch of rain on September 1st. This was only enough moisture to keep the county from degrading through the month as there was no other rainfall for the month. Condition were so bad that farmers could not begin to plant winter wheat until soil moisture returned. Soil moisture was rated poor to fair through the month of September. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions worsened during the month of September due to a continued lack of widespread beneficial rainfall. A majority of the region saw less than 50% of normal precipitation with a large area of the South Plains and extreme southwest Texas Panhandle receiving less than 10% of normal. This led to an expansion of D4 (exceptional drought) conditions across the southwest Texas Panhandle and southwestern South Plains. The D3 (extreme Drought) region remained relatively unchanged during the month encompassing the remainder of the extreme southwestern Texas Panhandle and the majority of the South Plains.   ||Temperatures in September averaged below normal, which helped to mitigate the lack of rainfall during the month. Dryland crops continued to suffer from this stretch of hot and dry weather in many parts of the region. Up to 41% of the cotton crop in Texas was reported as poor to very poor. Numerous fields that normally would have had maturing cotton crops were barren due to the loss of the crop. Cattle were reported to be in good condition despite the deteriorating pasture conditions due to supplemental feeding. Lack of rainfall was also delaying the planting of the winter wheat crop across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As Hurricane Beta traversed northward across the Gulf of Mexico in late September, outgoing swell propagated into the lower Texas coastal waters on September 20th through the 22nd. This swell was powerful enough to push water into the dunes across South Padre Island around high tide, which occurred at 7:30 PM CDT on the 20th and 9:37 PM CDT on the 21st. The highest level that was reported was 2.42 feet above Mean Higher-High Water (MHHW; or the level above normally dry ground at the shoreline) at 3:24 AM CDT on the 21st and 2.54 feet above MHHW at 11:24 PM CDT also on the 21st. Levels remained above 1.25 feet MHHW for several hours surrounding the occurrence of high tide on both evenings of September 20th through the 21st and again on the 21st through the 22nd. This activity was enough to cause some coastal erosion.||Farther up the coast, similar wave action and minor coastal flooding was likely - but there were no comparative measurements based on MHHW.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS survey of tidal run-up and overwash on South Padre Island found significant dune erosion at many of City and County beach access points between Isla Blanca Park and Clayton's Beach Bar.  Beta's water eroded more dunes that began with run-up associated with Hurricane Laura in late August. Though water levels during the time of the run-up (through the high tide cycle) were above 1.5 feet above Mean Higher High Water (MHHW), the strongest push of wave energy occurred at this time (high combined seas and long period swell from the east).

Tropical Storm Beta made landfall near Matagorda bay on September 21. As the system pushed slowly inland, portions of SE Texas experienced flash flooding from heavy rain bands, storm surge, and some strong wind gusts. Storm surge was enhanced by long fetch of northeast winds over Gulf behind a cold front. 20 to 25 homes were damaged from high water along portions of Clear Creek and Lower Brays Bayous.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant beach erosion occurred along the Bolivar Peninsula and Galveston Island. A fishing pier at 61st St. in Galveston was damaged. Numerous roads were flooded from high storm tides. Some garages were inundated with water. Maximum water heights reached 3.77 ft above MHHW at the Galveston Fishing Pier and 3.56 above MHHW at the Galveston Bay entrance.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread coastal flooding occurred across coastal Galveston County. Flooding was observed in San Leon, Bayou Vista, Clear Lake Shores, and Kemah. The Kemah Boardwalk was flooded. Numerous instances of street flooding and road closures were reported. Heavy rain from the storm resulted in bayou and creek flooding, including Clear Creek.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tide gage at Port Mansfield (inside Laguna Madre) recorded a peak value of 1.61 feet above Mean Sea Level (MSL) at 10:06 PM CST on September 20th.  This mini-high tide corresponded closely to high tide times along the coast farther south, and it is assumed that minor coastal flooding in the form of tidal run-up slightly into the dunes of the Willacy County Beaches near Port Mansfield occurred at this time. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tide gage at Port Mansfield (inside Laguna Madre) recorded a peak value of 1.39 feet above Mean Sea Level (MSL) at 7 AM CST on September 20th.  However, the location inside Laguna Madre was likely unrepresentative of conditions along the barrier island farther south, down to the Cameron County line.  An NWS survey on South Padre Island found significant beach erosion during the high tide cycle during the evening of the 19th, a result of strong landward push of high seas and swell. Therefore, times listed here are a proxy to similar impacts just 10 to 20 miles farther south on September 19th.

The month of November featured an extended period of dry weather, above normal temperatures and well below normal rainfall.  According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, the drought became severe over a large part of West Central Texas by the middle of November, especially south of Interstate 20.  The drought was exceptional in portions of McCulloch and Mason Counties.||According to the Agrlife Texas Crop and Weather Report, the area from Barnhart to San Saba and north across I20, needed moisture. Wheat and oat fields were progressing slowly. Stock tank levels remained very low. Cotton harvest continued with below average yields reported. Pecan harvest was mixed with some reporting low yields and others reporting fair to good yields. Feeder cattle prices were mostly steady at local auctions with steer prices up $10-15 per hundredweight. ||South of Barnhart to San Saba across I10, Trace amounts of rain were reported, but more rain was needed. Spring wheat planting began with oats and rye coming along nicely. Livestock conditions were good with supplemental feeding. Wildlife were in fair condition.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: U.S. Drought Monitor placed most of Runnels County in a severe drought category by November 17.

A very unstable airmass and some mid and upper level wind shear resulted in the development of isolated severe thunderstorms.  These reports led to some damaging wind and small hail reports across portions of Brown and Jones Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hawley Police Department reported lots of pea to dime size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures at the end of August extended into the first day of September. A Heat Advisory was in effect for the region, but parts of it were cancelled during the day due to thunderstorm outflow winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures at the end of August extended into the first day of September. A Heat Advisory was in effect for the region, but parts of it were cancelled during the day due to thunderstorm outflow winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across East Texas during the early morning hours on September 3rd, near a weak upper level trough extending from Central and Northeast Texas and Southwest Arkansas, with an associated upper low pressure system along this trough axis over East-Central Texas. A strong southerly low level jet within a very moist air mass in place contributed to large scale forcing enhancing thunderstorm development containing heavy rain near this trough and low pressure system, with these storms moving repeatedly over the same areas across portions of Smith, Gregg, Western Harrison, Upshur, and Wood Counties. Widespread rainfall amounts of 4-6 inches fell over these areas during the early morning hours of the 3rd, contributing to flooding of low lying, poor drainage areas in Central Longview. This necessitated the need for high water rescues in portions of Longview where motorists drove through these flood waters. These storms weakened by the mid-morning hours with the flood threat ending as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several areas in Longview were covered in high water, with water rescues needed due to drivers driving into the high water. These included West Cotton and South High Street, South Green Street and East Cotton Street, Tulip Lane, 303 Drake Street, South Green Street and Mopac Street, and Mobberly Avenue and South Methvin Road. These streets/intersections are all located in low lying, poor drainage areas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were impassable in Seabrook due to storm surge flooding, including Todville Rd, Hammer St, Main St, and 10th St. A fishing pier and kayak launch were damaged in Shoreacres. At Manchester, a maximum water level of 4.58 ft above MHHW was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As Hurricane Beta traversed northward across the Gulf of Mexico in late September, outgoing swell propagated into the lower Texas coastal waters on September 20th through the 22nd. This swell was powerful enough to push water into the dunes across South Padre Island around high tide, which occurred at 7:30 PM CDT on the 20th and 9:37 PM CDT on the 21st. The highest level that was reported was 2.42 feet above Mean Higher-High Water (MHHW; or the level above normally dry ground at the shoreline) at 3:24 AM CDT on the 21st and 2.54 feet above MHHW at 11:24 PM CDT also on the 21st. Levels remained above 1.25 feet MHHW for several hours surrounding the occurrence of high tide on both evenings of September 20th through the 21st and again on the 21st through the 22nd. This activity was enough to cause some coastal erosion.||Farther up the coast, similar wave action and minor coastal flooding was likely - but there were no comparative measurements based on MHHW.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tide gage at Port Mansfield (inside Laguna Madre) rose above 1.5 feet above Mean Sea Level (MSL) around 6:30 PM September 21, and continued well above 1.5 feet through the 23rd, peaking at 1.80 MSL at 3:24 PM on the 22nd. Though tide levels inside Laguna Madre remained high through the 23rd, the lack of landward push of water from Beta that day as the cyclone had already made landfall on the 22nd along the central Texas coast, well north of Port Mansfield, suggested that landward push of water had ended much earlier.  An NWS survey on South Padre Island found significant beach erosion during the high tide cycle during the evening of the 20th, a result of continuing strong landward push of high seas and swell. It was likely that additional minor erosion affected the northern coast of Willacy County into the high tide over September 21/22.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions worsened during the month of September due to a continued lack of widespread beneficial rainfall. A majority of the region saw less than 50% of normal precipitation with a large area of the South Plains and extreme southwest Texas Panhandle receiving less than 10% of normal. This led to an expansion of D4 (exceptional drought) conditions across the southwest Texas Panhandle and southwestern South Plains. The D3 (extreme Drought) region remained relatively unchanged during the month encompassing the remainder of the extreme southwestern Texas Panhandle and the majority of the South Plains.   ||Temperatures in September averaged below normal, which helped to mitigate the lack of rainfall during the month. Dryland crops continued to suffer from this stretch of hot and dry weather in many parts of the region. Up to 41% of the cotton crop in Texas was reported as poor to very poor. Numerous fields that normally would have had maturing cotton crops were barren due to the loss of the crop. Cattle were reported to be in good condition despite the deteriorating pasture conditions due to supplemental feeding. Lack of rainfall was also delaying the planting of the winter wheat crop across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough shifted southeast across the Southern Plains and into East Texas and Southwest Arkansas during the evening hours of September 27th, which reinforced the first fall cold front of the season southeast into the Ark-La-Tex from the late evening hours through the morning hours of September 28th. A strong southerly low level flow ahead of the front resulted in a rapid increase in low level moisture north across East Texas, Southeast Oklahoma, and Southwest Arkansas ahead of the front, while strong large scale forcing increased near this boundary within an unstable air mass. As a result, a cluster of strong thunderstorms developed near and just ahead of the front, with one storm briefly becoming severe in Red River County Texas, where quarter size hail was reported between Bogota and Clarksville. These storms weakened as they spread southeast across East Texas, Southern Arkansas, and North Louisiana during the early morning hours of the 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell on Highway 37 between Bogota and Clarksville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tide gage at Baffin Bay, just north of the Kenedy/Kleberg County line, peaked at 2.34 feet above Mean Sea Level (MSL) around 8 PM CST September 21, and continued above 2 feet above MSL through 5 PM CST on the 23rd. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Beta began as a low pressure area over the southwest Gulf of Mexico on September 16th. This system organized into a tropical depression on the 17th as it moved slowly northward. It became Tropical Storm Beta on the 18th while moving north into the northwest Gulf of Mexico. It became stationary on the 19th as steering flow collapsed. Beta moved on a slow and steady course to the west-northwest toward the Middle Texas coast on the 20th and 21st and made landfall on the southern end of the Matagorda Peninsula late on the evening of 21st. With the slow movement of Beta and higher astronomical tides, a long duration coastal flood event occurred from the 19th to early on the 23rd, with 2 to 3.5 feet of inundation occurring along the Middle Texas coast. The high tides and surge flooded several roads in Corpus Christi, Aransas Pass, Rockport, Fulton, Port O'Connor, Indianola, and Port Lavaca. Locally heavy rainfall occurred during the early morning hours of the 22nd over eastern Victoria County that produced flash flooding in Inez.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tide levels ranged from 2 to 3.5 feet of inundation during the period.

Tropical Storm Beta began as a low pressure area over the southwest Gulf of Mexico on September 16th. This system organized into a tropical depression on the 17th as it moved slowly northward. It became Tropical Storm Beta on the 18th while moving north into the northwest Gulf of Mexico. It became stationary on the 19th as steering flow collapsed. Beta moved on a slow and steady course to the west-northwest toward the Middle Texas coast on the 20th and 21st and made landfall on the southern end of the Matagorda Peninsula late on the evening of 21st. With the slow movement of Beta and higher astronomical tides, a long duration coastal flood event occurred from the 19th to early on the 23rd, with 2 to 3.5 feet of inundation occurring along the Middle Texas coast. The high tides and surge flooded several roads in Corpus Christi, Aransas Pass, Rockport, Fulton, Port O'Connor, Indianola, and Port Lavaca. Locally heavy rainfall occurred during the early morning hours of the 22nd over eastern Victoria County that produced flash flooding in Inez.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tide levels ranged from 2 to 3 feet of inundation during the period. Ocean Drive Causeway was closed due to high water between Texas A&M Corpus Christi and NAS Corpus Christi. Access roads to South Padre Island Drive were flooded between Flour Bluff and North Padre Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought cold air to South Central Texas on December 30 and then stalled along the coast. On the 31st an area of low pressure developed along the front as an upper level trough moved from northern Mexico into Texas. This combination led to the development of snow showers across the northern part of the area. Snow accumulation was reported in Burnet, Edwards, Gillespie, Kerr, Llano, and Real Counties ranging from 0.2 inches to 4.5 inches. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought continued over most of South Central Texas in December. Most of the area had less than normal precipitation during the month. Drought conditions worsened from Severe (D2) to Extreme (D3) in Caldwell, Guadalupe, Kinney, Val Verde, and Williamson Counties and from Moderate (D1) to D2 in Fayette County. Bandera, Comal, Gillespie, Kendall, Kerr, Uvalde, and Zavala Counties remained in D3 and Bastrop, Bexar, Blanco, Burnet, Dimmit, Edwards, Frio, Hays, Lee, Llano, Maverick, Medina, Real, and Travis remained in D2. Large public water systems in Austin, Del Rio, Fredericksburg, Kerrville, New Braunfels, San Antonio, San Marcos, and Uvalde had some level of water restrictions. Most area reservoirs were below Conservation Pool level. Lake Amistad was 43.3 feet below, Medina Lake 32.2 feet, and Lake Travis 21.8 feet. Outdoor burn bans were in effect in Bandera, Bexar, Dimmit, Edwards, Frio, Kendall, Kinney, Maverick, Medina, Real, Val Verde, and Zavala Counties.

A potent, but moisture starved, upper level storm system traversed the South Plains region on the morning of the 13th. As the system passed it carried in colder air on gusty northerly winds. In addition, it was able to squeeze out a quick shot of moisture, primarily across the southern Texas Panhandle. Temperatures cooled enough that most of this precipitation fell in the form of snow. The system tracked steadily east-southeastward through the day. Most of the snow fell during the morning hours. The wintry precipitation quickly diminished and shifted east with the system during the afternoon hours.||Snowfall reports are from NWS cooperative weather observers and public reports:||2.1 inches at Tulia (Swisher County), 2.0 inches at Memphis (Hall County), 1.5 inches at Silverton (Briscoe County), 1.0 inch at Floydada (Floyd County), Childress (Childress County), Vigo Park (Swisher County), and Hart (Castro  County).
